title: The Supreme Court Picks the Wrong Patent Fight in Hikma v. Amarin - IPWatchdog.com
content:
“In choosing Hikma v. Amarin, the Court has focused on a narrow question aligned with current concerns about drug pricing—while leaving unresolved deeper uncertainties in patent law that affect innovation across the entire economy.” The Supreme Court’s decision to hear Hikma Pharmaceuticals USA Inc. v. Amarin Pharma, Inc. is puzzling—and revealing. On its face, the case presents a narrow question: whether a generic drug manufacturer can be held liable for inducing patent infringement based on how it markets a product approved under a so-called “skinny label.” The dispute turns on whether Hikma’s conduct plausibly encouraged physicians to prescribe its generic drug for a patented use. But the Court’s decision to grant certiorari reflects something broader: a continued focus on lowering drug prices through faster generic entry, even at the risk of undermining the patent incentives that make pharmaceutical innovation possible. For those who practice in this space, the concern is not simply doctrinal. It is structural. Since 1984, pharmaceutical competition has been shaped by the Hatch-Waxman Act—a statute designed to balance innovation and generic entry. The premise was straightforward: generics would enter efficiently after patent expiration, while patent term restoration and regulatory exclusivities would preserve incentives for innovation. In practice, that balance has proven far more difficult to maintain. Rather than a streamlined system, Hatch-Waxman has produced a dense legal environment characterized by strategic behavior on all sides. Brand manufacturers develop portfolios of follow-on patents and pursue lifecycle management strategies. Generic firms leverage regulatory mechanisms such as the 180-day exclusivity period. Both sides rely heavily on litigation and settlement—often determining the timing of competition through negotiated outcomes rather than judicial resolution. The result is a system that is not only complex, but highly endogenous. Legal rules do not simply govern behavior; they become inputs into strategic decision-making. This dynamic reflects a deeper limitation. Efforts to design legal frameworks that precisely calibrate innovation incentives and competition must contend with the reality that firms will adapt in ways that lawmakers cannot fully anticipate. The more intricate the regulatory scheme, the more opportunities it creates for strategic behavior. Hikma raises an important question about the proper scope of inducement liability in this already complex framework. Petitioners urge the Court to adopt a rule that would effectively insulate generic manufacturers from inducement liability so long as their labeling formally excludes patented uses. Under this view, compliance with FDA-approved labeling would operate as a de facto safe harbor—even if other aspects of a manufacturer’s conduct encourage infringing uses. That approach would mark a significant departure from traditional patent principles. Inducement liability has long turned on real-world conduct—what a defendant says, does, and intends—not merely on formal compliance with regulatory categories. Courts have treated inducement as a fact-intensive inquiry precisely because market behavior cannot be reduced to labels alone. Replacing that inquiry with a bright-line rule tied to labeling would collapse inducement doctrine into a formalistic test, potentially allowing sophisticated actors to structure their conduct to avoid liability while still capturing the value of patented innovations. For patent practitioners, the concern is straightforward: such a rule would introduce a new form of uncertainty by decoupling liability from actual marketplace behavior. The stakes of this doctrinal shift are particularly high in the pharmaceutical context. Amarin’s drug Vascepa provides a clear example. Initially approved for a narrow indication, the drug later proved highly effective in reducing the risk of cardiovascular events—a discovery that required substantial additional investment and clinical research. That second indication is now the drug’s primary source of clinical value. Post-approval innovation of this kind is critical to modern medicine. It reflects the reality that the full therapeutic potential of a drug is often discovered only after it reaches the market. Patent protection plays a central role in supporting these investments. If generic manufacturers can appropriate the value of newly discovered uses through carefully structured marketing—while relying on formal compliance with labeling requirements—then the incentive to undertake such research is weakened. Much of the policy discourse surrounding pharmaceutical markets focuses on accelerating generic entry as a means of reducing prices. Generic competition lowers prices only after innovation has occurred. It does not generate new therapies. Indeed, weakening the mechanisms that allow innovators to capture the value of their discoveries risks reducing the investment that makes future competition possible. A more durable form of competition comes from innovation itself: the development of new drugs within a therapeutic class and entirely new mechanisms of action. That form of competition expands patient choice and treats more diseases while exerting significant downward pressure on prices over time. Legal rules that erode innovation incentives may produce short-term pricing effects, but at the cost of long-term progress. If the Supreme Court wishes to address the foundations of pharmaceutical innovation, it has had ample opportunity to do so. For years, litigants have sought clarification of the Court’s patent subject matter eligibility jurisprudence under Section 101. The current doctrine remains widely criticized as unpredictable and difficult to apply—particularly in the context of diagnostic technologies. That uncertainty has real consequences. Many cutting-edge therapies, especially in oncology, depend on companion diagnostics to identify which patients will benefit most. When patent protection for such diagnostics is unclear or unavailable, investment in their development becomes significantly more risky. The Federal Circuit has struggled to provide consistent guidance in this area, and the Supreme Court has repeatedly declined to intervene. Clarifying the scope of patent eligibility—particularly for diagnostics—would do far more to support innovation than further refining the timing of generic entry under Hatch-Waxman. The deeper issue is not limited to inducement doctrine or Hatch-Waxman. It reflects a broader tendency to treat innovation markets as systems that can be engineered through increasingly detailed legal rules. Economist F.A. Hayek warned against what he called the “fatal conceit”—the belief that policymakers can design complex social systems whose operation depends on knowledge they do not possess. Pharmaceutical innovation is shaped by uncertainty, dispersed knowledge, and continual adaptation. Firms respond to incentives in ways that are difficult to predict ex ante. Legal frameworks can support this process, but they cannot precisely control it. Efforts to do so often produce unintended consequences—greater complexity, more litigation, and new forms of strategic behavior. Recognizing these limits is essential to designing a patent system that promotes long-term innovation rather than short-term optimization. The Supreme Court’s docket is limited. Each case reflects a judgment about which issues deserve attention. In choosing Hikma v. Amarin, the Court has focused on a narrow question aligned with current concerns about drug pricing—while leaving unresolved deeper uncertainties in patent law that affect innovation across the entire economy. For patent practitioners, the concern is clear. The Court is engaging at the margins while leaving core doctrinal instability untouched. If the goal is to bring about lower pharmaceutical prices, the priority should be promoting meaningful competition through continued innovation. That comes mostly from stronger, more predictable patent rights—not by tinkering with rules that risk further destabilizing a highly complex system that has largely failed to deliver. The Court has had numerous opportunities to properly address that goal. Image Source: Deposit Photos

title: Schmidt v. City of Norfolk Brief: Automated License Plate Readers Commit Fourth Amendment Searches - Cato Institute
content: Sign up to have blog posts delivered straight to your inbox! Norfolk, Virginia, has deployed nearly 200 automated license plate readers (ALPRs) across the city, capturing every passing vehicle’s location, time, and identifying details—and storing that data for weeks. Now, two residents are suing, arguing that this sweeping, warrantless tracking of their daily movements amounts to unconstitutional surveillance under the Fourth Amendment. ALPRs “are high-speed, computer-controlled camera systems” that capture all license plate numbers, along with the location, date, and time. Norfolk has deployed advanced ALPRs from the technology company Flock that use artificial intelligence to record the make, type, color, and distinctive features of a vehicle (like bumper stickers) to create a trackable “Vehicle Fingerprint.” They are capable of revealing how fast and in what direction a person traveled and can even reconstruct a vehicle’s historical routes over time. In just a matter of keystrokes, officers can chart a person’s full movement across a city over a specific timeframe, with almost no restrictions or oversight. The ALPRs installed by the City of Norfolk automatically record every vehicle that passes by and archive that information for 21 days. The Appellants in this case—Lee Schmidt and Crystal Arrington—are Norfolk residents whose cars are captured by the city’s ALPRs, sometimes several times per day. They sued, alleging that the city’s warrantless collection of their movements throughout the city—and retention of those records for future review and inspection—violates the Fourth Amendment. The district court dismissed their complaint, holding that the ALPRs do not violate the Fourth Amendment because they do not track “the whole” of their movements. Although the city’s ALPRs are stationary and do not persistently track people the way a cell phone or a drone can, the public nature of a roadway is not a blank check for government surveillance. The Supreme Court once held that a person has no expectation of privacy in “movements from one place to another,” but it has since refined that analysis. As technology has advanced, the Court has recognized that even data collected in public spaces can offer an intimate window into a person’s life—revealing where they travel, how often, and for how long—and that “more sophisticated” surveillance methods can violate the Fourth Amendment. Norfolk’s ALPR network qualifies as such a system. Cato has filed an amicus brief to highlight two points. First, the Fourth Amendment was designed to eliminate indiscriminate searches and the threat of “general warrants” that allowed British officials to rummage through Americans’ homes and papers. Today’s technology poses an analogous threat: with no probable cause, government officials can now collect and store intimate details of a person’s life—including financial information and location information—for perusal at the click of a button. Without judicial oversight, tools like ALPRs give police the discretionary power to conduct suspicionless searches that the Framers sought to prohibit. Second, the district court’s rule—accepting all government tracking short of the very most exhaustive kind—would invite the warrantless deployment of many technologies to surveil the public. Modern surveillance tools such as ALPRs, military-grade drones, financial “audit trails,” and other technologies and sensors let the government monitor millions of people with ease, tracking movements, detecting faces, recording digital conversations—then storing everything for later review. Left unchecked, such tools would render the Fourth Amendment’s warrant requirement a dead letter. Cato has asked the Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals to reverse the decision and to foreclose a surveillance apparatus that silently records the daily movements of every Norfolk resident who ventures onto a public street. Every weekday morning: independent analysis grounded in individual liberty, limited government, free markets, and peace.

title: Xbox Delivers April’s Second Game Pass Wave, Stacking Final Fantasy V, Aphelion, Trepang2, and More - Wccftech
content: Xbox has revealed the second batch of games joining Xbox Game Pass for the remainder of April 2026, and it's a much more solid wave of entries compared to the first batch, if only because the games listed are actually new entries to the service and not just a bunch of games previously already available at the Game Pass Ultimate tier moving down to Premium. The new entries include a wave of games launching directly into Xbox Game Pass, including titles previously announced like Double Fine's upcoming Kiln and Poncle's new Vampire Crawlers. It also includes Aphelion, the upcoming sci-fi adventure from developer DON'T NOD, which will launch into Game Pass next week. It's a game that could act as an adrenaline boost for DON'T NOD, as it appears to be one of the developer's most intriguing games yet. You can learn more about it in our preview and conversation with the team here. Other day-one entries include multiplayer title Sledding Game and the early access launch of Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era. Not a day-one launch, but still notable are the inclusions of Final Fantasy V joining the first four Final Fantasy games, and first-person shooter Trepang2 joining the subscription service. Here's the full list of games joining Xbox Game Pass for the remainder of April 2026, when they're joining, what platforms they'll be available on, and which subscription tiers will have access to them. On top of these games entering the service, about just as many are set to leave the service at the end of the month. Nine games will exit the subscription service on April 30, 2026, including a few heavy-hitting inclusions like the original Citizen Sleeper and Revenge of the Savage Planet. Here's the full list of what is leaving as of April 30: For more on Game Pass, check out our hub article that includes every single game available in the subscription service, which we keep updated with every new announcement of games entering and leaving. title: Harger: With harm reduction v. sobriety, King County picked a side. The science says the debate isn't over - MyNorthwest.com
content: Apr 20, 2026, 8:44 AM                        | Updated: 12:53 pm Art Dahlen from Battlefield Addiction came into the video studio this week. If you’ve been listening to this show, you know Art. Recovering addict. Works with families. Fights like hell to get people clean. Doesn’t sugarcoat anything. Max is one of Art’s right-hand people at Battlefield now. He was a former NFL wide receiver who got trapped by painkillers. It took a lot of tries to get him out, but he’s been clean for years. You notice it immediately. Quiet confidence. Doesn’t say much. Doesn’t need to. You can tell he’s seen some very dark places and came back. He’s ready to help pull others out. Then there was Kenny. Kenny got clean four months ago. He’s timid. A little unsure. This is the first time he’s been thinking clearly in years. He has the look of someone who spent a long time in a dark tunnel and is just now stepping into the sunlight. Every day is a fight. He’s taking it one day at a time. If you’ve heard my interviews with Art over the past year, you’ve heard Adam’s voice. He told me about shoplifting hundreds of dollars a day to feed his habit. He was at Art’s side for just about every conversation we’ve had on this program. Adam relapsed recently. He was just in a bad car crash. The outlook is uncertain. That’s the reality of this. Max made it back. Kenny is making it back. Adam is fighting for his life. Recovery isn’t a straight line, and Art knows that better than anyone. He doesn’t promise easy. He promises a door. Not everyone walks through it the first time. But the door has to exist. Art’s model is built around one idea. Sobriety. Not managed addiction. Not comfortable using. Sobriety. Independence. A life that belongs to you again. Under the harm reduction model, the approach isn’t to proactively offer detox or treatment. It’s to wait until the user asks. In the meantime, make the use as comfortable as possible. Keep someone alive one more day, and maybe today’s the day they decide they want out. The heart is in the right place. But there’s a difference between keeping someone alive and helping them live. Fentanyl killed 1,996 people in Washington last year. More than five people a day. And we point to that number as progress because it was six a day the year before. One fewer body. That’s the metric. Art doesn’t measure success in bodies. He measures it in people like Max. People like Kenny. People who are standing in the sunlight, thinking clearly, building something. That takes longer than handing someone supplies. It’s harder. It requires accountability. It requires the person to want it and the system to demand it. The harm reduction model keeps people alive. Art’s model tries to give them their lives back. Those aren’t the same thing. And I’m not saying harm reduction has no place. For some people, in some circumstances, keeping someone alive long enough to want something different is exactly the right move. Fine. But around here in Seattle, it’s the only move. The only acceptable answer. The only approved approach. And we’ve been all in on it for years. The tents are still there. The bodies are still piling up. At some point, you have to ask: At what point do we try something different? If you question any of this in King County, you run into a wall. Not just disagreement. Dismissal. The experts have decided. Harm reduction is the science. Everything else is cruelty dressed up as tough love. The conversation is over. A peer-reviewed meta-analysis published in Campbell Systematic Reviews in April 2024 examined 48 studies involving more than 15,000 participants. Its conclusion: Abstinence-based and harm reduction-based interventions “make little real difference to the outcomes achieved in comparison to treatment as usual.” The confidence intervals for both approaches cross zero. Neither dramatically outperforms the other. That’s not my opinion. That’s from one of the most comprehensive reviews of the evidence published to date. The research doesn’t declare a winner. It says the debate is unresolved. So when King County tells you harm reduction is settled science and the sobriety model is cruel, they’re not following the research. They picked a side and locked the door on the other one. What’s particularly frustrating is the certainty of it. As if the people running these programs have looked at the results, the tents, the open drug scenes, the bodies, and concluded the only problem is that we haven’t done enough of the same thing. Meanwhile, the people with actual lived experience get shown the door. Recovering addicts who know what pulled them back from the edge. People like Art, who got clean, who helps others get clean, and who can show you Max and Kenny as proof that another way works. Their experience doesn’t count because it doesn’t fit the framework. They’re dismissed as anecdotes. As outliers. As people who got lucky. One path to recovery is being dismissed entirely in favor of another. And the results are right in front of us. What harm reduction has produced in King County is a lot of people kept alive but going nowhere. Warehoused in supportive housing with no expectation of recovery. Rotting in tents, day after day, in the middle of their addiction. Not getting better. Not building anything. Just existing. The system calls that compassion. There’s no strong evidence that harm reduction alone produces lasting recovery. What it produces is managed decline. And somewhere in that decline are people who could be living independently, thinking clearly, building a life. If anyone bothered to offer them the door Art holds open. Kenny got clean four months ago. He’s standing in the sunlight for the first time in years. Not because the system handed him supplies and left him alone. Because someone demanded more of him than that, and he rose to meet it. If Kenny were your son, which would you want the system to offer him? A straw and some foil, or a way out? Charlie Harger is the host of “Seattle’s Morning News” on KIRO Newsradio. You can read more of his stories and commentaries here. Follow Charlie on X and email him here. According to King County, harm reduction is the science. Everything else is cruelty dressed up as tough love. The conversation is over. Except the science doesn't actually say that. title: Shield AI selected by U.S. Navy to compete for $800M in ISR services with V-BAT - Shield AI
content:
Autonomy. Faster.

Develop, evaluate, test and deploy mission autonomy with the most comprehensive AI-powered developer platform. Our team, your platform.

Shield AI engineers provide rapid deployment and adaptation to your mission. Post-flight debrief evaluate, score, and visualize mission critical data Unrivaled surveillance, day or night, across air, land and sea. AI-powered software for superior object detection in videos Transform your EO/IR camera into a silent drone hunter Our mission is to protect service members and civilians with intelligent systems. Brilliant people drive innovation and contribute to a mission with massive impact. At Shield AI, we're not just building software; we're revolutionizing defense technology. WASHINGTON — (April 20, 2026) — Shield AI announced today its selection by the United States Navy to provide contractor-owned, contractor-operated (COCO) intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) services in support of naval and joint force operations. Under the Navy’s initiative to expand and modernize ISR capabilities, Shield AI will compete for up to $800 million in task orders alongside other selected industry partners, delivering persistent ISR using its V-BAT vertical take-off and landing (VTOL) uncrewed aircraft system (UAS). “V-BAT has delivered more operational outcomes than any other Group 3 VTOL UAS. We’ve interdicted over 100,000 lbs of narcotics in the Caribbean and Pacific. V-BAT has executed hundreds of targeting operations in Ukraine, where GPS and communications are jammed during every mission, and we have delivered substantial outcomes in the Middle East and with our allies and partners,” said Brandon Tseng, Shield AI’s co-founder, president and a former Navy Surface Warfare Officer and SEAL. “We aren’t just bringing the V-BAT product and service to the Navy; we’re bringing a world-class team with a wealth of operational experience and the ability to produce undeniable outcomes for our warfighters.” V-BAT is a Group 3 vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL) uncrewed aircraft system with a ducted-fan design, more than 12 hours of endurance, and a heavy-fuel engine. It is actively operating at sea and from land-based sites with the U.S. Coast Guard and U.S. Marine Corps, including deployments from Navy ships. Designed for contested electronic warfare environments, V-BAT’s single-engine, enclosed-rotor configuration enables safe, unassisted launch and recovery from ship decks and austere locations, supporting persistent ISR with a small, highly expeditionary footprint and minimal logistics requirements. Distribution Statement A: Approved for Public Release; NAVAIR 2026-0267 Founded in 2015, Shield AI is a venture-backed deep-tech company with the mission of protecting service members and civilians with intelligent systems. Its products include the V-BAT and X-BAT aircraft, Hivemind Enterprise, and the Hivemind Vision product lines. With offices and facilities across the U.S., Europe, the Middle East, and the Asia-Pacific, Shield AI’s technology actively supports operations worldwide. For more information, visit www.shield.ai. Follow Shield AI on LinkedIn, X, Instagram, and YouTube. Learn more about how we’re revolutionizing autonomy. Our mission is to protect service members and civilians with intelligent systems.

title: Debrief: Low-Cost Missile Craze Targets Block V Tomahawk Role - Aviation Week
content:
This article is published in
           Aerospace Daily & Defense Report part of Aviation Week Intelligence
          Network (AWIN), and is complimentary through
          May 22, 2026. For information on becoming an AWIN Member to
          access more content like this, click here. NATIONAL HARBOR, Maryland—A multitude of options exist to hit a slow-moving ship: mines, torpedoes, short-range missiles, midrange cruise missiles, etc. But the options narrow for the U.S. military to only one if the ship needs to be attacked from beyond 1,000 nm away: the Raytheon BGM-109 Block V Maritime Strike Tomahawk (MST), a subsonic, non-stealthy cruise missile launched by destroyers, submarines and the U.S. Army’s new Typhon system. Except for the Netherlands, which was authorized to import the MST cruise missile last year, foreign militaries don’t have any options. But that could be changing soon. The U.S. Air Force launched market research for the Family of Affordable Mass Missiles-Beyond Adversary’s Reach (FAMM-BAR) on April 17. “The potential procurement objective is to produce an inventory for the [U.S.] Government and Foreign Military Sales. The expectation is that the annual production orders will range from 1,000-2,000 units per year for five years (procurement numbers will vary by year),” the Air Force Life Cycle Management Center’s Weapons Capacity Task Force says in the request for information. The FAMM-BAR program lists five desired attributes for the potential weapon: a range of 1,000 nm or more, a speed of at least 0.7 Mach, palletized delivery from a cargo aircraft, the ability to receive midcourse navigation updates and the manufacturing capacity to produce more than 1,000 missiles a year. The main target set for the weapon is “slow moving maritime” vessels. The FAMM-BAR nomenclature makes the program a new international and longer-range variant of the FAMM initiative, which is already developing FAMM-Palletized and FAMM-Lugged cruise missiles for the U.S. Air Force for large-scale production. By contrast, FAMM-P and FAMM-L require ranges of 250-500 nm. The extended range of the FAMM-BAR also distinguishes the weapon from a similar effort by the U.S. Navy. The Coalition Heterogeneous Affordable Offensive Strike (CHAOS) program, launched in February, is seeking a medium-range, low-cost cruise missile to provide to international partners. The demand from the Air Force and Navy has spawned a new industry. Anduril, CoAspire and Zone 5 Technologies are involved in the FAMM-P and FAMM-L programs. The latter two companies also are producing similar Extended Range Attack Missiles for Ukraine. CoAspire unveiled a new version of its FAMM contender—the Rapidly Adaptable Affordable Cruise Missile (RAACM)—at the Navy League’s Sea Air Space conference here on April 20. The RAACM-ER is advertised with range greater than 1,000 nm. Other options are also in development. Last year, Global Technical Systems, a Virginia-based company, unveiled a project to build a 1,200-nm cruise missile with an “anti-maritime warhead." Steve covers military aviation, missiles and space for the Aviation Week Network, based in Washington, DC. Get free defense news, insight and analysis from our award-winning editors delivered to your inbox daily.

title: Pick of the stats: Birmingham City v Preston North End - Yahoo Sports
content: Birmingham City and Preston North End will look to end on a positive in their final three games of the season despite being out of contention at both ends of the Championship table. The two sides meet at St Andrew's on Wednesday night (kick-off 19:45 BST) with neither able to finish in a play-off race or be relegated. Blues have won just one of their past six games (D2 L3) but have at least stayed up following promotion from League One last season. The Lilywhites had their four-game unbeaten run (W2 D2) ended by a 2-0 home defeat to West Bromwich Albion on Saturday. Birmingham have won their past two league games against Preston, and are looking to complete the double over the Lilywhites for the first time since 2000-01. Preston have lost 1-0 in their past two league games against Birmingham. They last lost three in a row without scoring against the Blues between 1925 and 1935. Birmingham have won 10 of their 21 home league games this season (D8 L3); they last recorded more home league wins in a second tier campaign in 2011-12 (13). Preston are looking to win successive away league matches for the second time this season, previously doing so in November/December at Sheffield Wednesday (3-2) and Oxford (2-1). Lewis Dobbin has been involved in nine away league goals for Preston this season (4 goals, 5 assists); the last player to reach doubles figures for them in a Championship campaign was Tom Barkhuizen in 2017-18 (11).

title: Pick of the stats: West Bromwich Albion v Watford - Yahoo Sports
content: West Bromwich Albion will hope to secure their Championship status for another season when they host Watford on Wednesday night (kick-off 19:45 BST). The Baggies are 20th and five points clear of 22nd-placed Oxford United with three games to play. If Albion win and the U's fail to do so at home to Wrexham, James Morrison's side will be mathematically safe - though there is still a potential points deduction for breaching financial rules which could change the final standings. West Brom are undefeated in their past eight league games, while Watford, who cannot reach the play-offs or go down, are without a win in five (D2 L3). West Brom have won just one of their past 10 league games against Watford (D4 L5), though it was in this exact fixture last season (2-1). Following their 2-1 win in October, Watford are looking to complete the league double over West Brom for the first time since 1994-95. No side has drawn more home games in the Championship this season than West Brom (9), with five of their past seven league matches at the Hawthorns ending all square (W1 L1). Watford have lost each of their past three away league games, one more defeat than in their previous 12 league matches on their travels (W4 D6). Watford's Imran Louza has been involved in nine league goals away from home this season (4 goals, 5 assists); the last player for the Hornets to reach 10 in a campaign was Troy Deeney in 2018-19 (6 goals, 4 assists).

title: High-end seafood-steakhouse restaurant opens in Palm Beach Gardens - The Palm Beach Post
content: Palm Beach Gardens is about to get a new excuse to dress up. Eddie V’s Prime Seafood, the polished, high-energy steak-and-seafood restaurant equally known for its theatrical cocktails and live music lounge opens April 26 at Legacy Place. It marks the brand’s first Palm Beach Gardens location and its second in Palm Beach County, joining the longtime Boca Raton outpost. If you’ve been to Eddie V’s before, the formula will feel familiar. The dining room leans sleek and clubby, while the adjoining V Lounge sets the mood with dim lighting, a nightly rotation of live music and a menu designed to stretch the evening well past dinner. When I visited the Boca Raton restaurant, the scene felt a bit Vegas-y a la Venetian without the chaos. It’s luxe, lively and unapologetically indulgent. The menu centers on seafood flown in from around the world alongside hand-carved prime steaks prepared by an in-house butcher. Signature dishes include Chilean sea bass steamed Hong Kong-style, Parmesan-crusted sole and the crowd-pleasing crab fried rice. Steaks are cut daily and broiled to order, reinforcing Eddie V’s positioning as a hybrid steakhouse and seafood destination rather than an either-or proposition. Dessert is very much part of the show. Bananas Foster butter cake arrives flambéed tableside, finished with butter pecan ice cream and enough spectacle to pause nearby conversations. All desserts are baked daily in the restaurant’s pastry kitchen, a detail the brand likes to emphasize and one that regulars tend to appreciate. Cocktails are another headliner. In the V Lounge, the “Cocktails With Attitude” menu leans into presentation without losing its footing. The Smoked Old Fashioned is torched tableside, with smoke trapped in the glass before pouring. The Hope Diamond cocktail shifts color from deep blue to vivid magenta, a visual trick that reliably draws phones out of handbags. Wine lovers will notice the walk-through glass wine tower, stocked with more than 300 bottles, including Champagne and a deep bench of steakhouse-friendly reds. The list at other Eddie V's locations has earned recognition from Wine Spectator and is designed to serve celebratory dinners as comfortably as expense-account martinis at the bar. The Palm Beach Gardens restaurant is led by Managing Partner Heather Houston, who brings 25 years of industry experience and a local résumé that includes Darden siblings Seasons 52 and most recently The Capital Grille in Palm Beach Gardens. Houston is excited to introduce Eddie V’s to a community she knows well and to create what she calls a glamorous night-out destination anchored by fresh seafood, cocktails and live music. Beyond the dining room, the opening carries an economic footprint. The restaurant is expected to create roughly 75 new jobs spanning front- and back-of-house roles. For Palm Beach Gardens, Eddie V’s arrives not just as another restaurant opening, but as a bid for buzz. Call it dinner with a little drama and a lot of polish. Hours: Opens nightly at 4 p.m. Sunday through Thursday to 9 p.m. and Friday and Saturday to 10:00 p.m. V Lounge happy hour Sunday through Thursday from 4 to 6:30 p.m. Live music on Sunday from 6 to 9 p.m., Monday through Thursday from 6:30 to 9:30 p.m. and Friday and Saturday from 6:30 to 10 p.m. Complimentary valet available. Reservations are recommended but not required. Etc.: Dress code is business casual or resort evening wear. The following are not permitted: athletic shorts, uncovered sports bras, other bra-style tops, excessively revealing clothing, exposed undergarments, clothing with offensive language or imagery and clothing emitting offensive odors. Men’s shirts must have sleeves. Diana Biederman is the Palm Beach Post’s food and dining reporter who believes the best high-end deal in town is The Capital Grille's prix-fixe lunch. Reach me at dbiederman@pbpost.com. Subscribe today and sign up for our free At the Table weekly newsletter.

title: Pa. lieutenant governor Republican primary: Jason Richey v. John Ventre - 90.5 WESA
content: What's at stake: Candidates for lieutenant governor don’t start out with a running mate: They have to run in primary races of their own to join their party’s ticket in the fall. So while Pennsylvania Lt. Gov. Austin Davis faces no rival from Democrats, Republican voters have a choice when deciding who to pair with presumptive GOP gubernatorial nominee Stacy Garrity. Garrity has already expressed a preference for Jason Richey, an attorney and former gubernatorial candidate who leads the Republican Committee of Allegheny County. And while other potential GOP candidates backed out after Garrity announced her support, John Ventre stayed in. The anti-establishment candidate and retired UPS executive says he’s pushing for grassroots support in the May 19 primary. The lieutenant governor’s most obvious job is to assume the duties of leading the state should the governor die, resign, or be removed or otherwise incapacitated. The lieutenant governor also presides over the state Senate and can cast a tiebreaking vote on matters in which the 50-member body is evenly divided. Duties also include serving on the state’s Emergency Management Council and chairing the state’s Board of Pardons, which makes recommendations to the governor about pardon applications. Governors also frequently appoint their lieutenants to other state boards and commissions. Lieutenant governors serve four-year terms alongside their running mate. The position will pay $213,248 this year. Pittsburgh-area attorney and Allegheny County GOP Chairman Jason Richey, who previously ran for governor in 2022, was backed this year by the state Republican Party for lieutenant governor amid a small group of candidates. Richey says his campaign came about quickly after GOP gubernatorial candidate Stacy Garrity called him to extend an endorsement. Allegheny County holds the state’s largest number of Republican voters in the state, and Garrity, a Bradford County native, will need them to topple Democrat Shapiro, who’s seen as the favorite going into his reelection bid. Richey says he’s a tough attorney who put himself through law school in part by working at a steel mill in Beaver County. And he says he’s closely aligned with Garrity’s own agenda. Garrity backs Allegheny GOP leader Jason Richey as running mate, but Republican voters will decide (Tom Riese, WESA) Allegheny County Republicans choose Jason Richey as local chair while rivals question process (Chris Potter, WESA) Place of residence: Sewickley Heights, Allegheny County Education: B.A., economics and political science, Allegheny College; J.D., The Ohio State University Moritz College of Law Related experience: Thirty years as an attorney; chairman of the Republican Committee of Allegheny County Supporters/endorsements: The Republican Party of Pennsylvania; state Treasurer and GOP gubernatorial candidate Stacy Garrity; state Auditor General Tim DeFoor; Gun Owners of America; Pennsylvania Manufacturers’ Association PAC; several Republican U.S. and state House members; sheriffs for Butler and Beaver counties; and Republican county leaders in Chester and Westmoreland Total raised: $19,032.08, plus $8,000 the candidate loaned to the committeeTotal spent: $19,032.08 Why do you think you will be an effective running mate in terms of helping your party's candidate for governor win this November? Stacy Garrity and I share the same values and reasons for why we’re seeking the offices of Governor and Lieutenant Governor. For the past 12 years, Pennsylvania has been burdened by rising taxes, increased spending and policies that have made it more expensive and less prosperous to live here. Together, Stacy and I will campaign in every corner of the Commonwealth on our belief that we can and must do better by making Pennsylvania more affordable and creating more opportunities for individuals and job creators to thrive. Our efforts will start on Day 1 by unleashing Pennsylvania’s enormous energy potential and working to reduce the tax burden on taxpayers. If you are part of a winning gubernatorial ticket this fall, there is a chance that you may have to ascend to the post of governor should your gubernatorial nominee have to vacate it. What in your background prepares you for that responsibility? I grew up in Aliquippa, where I saw firsthand the impact of economic decline and learned the value of hard work. As a three-time NCAA Academic All-American wrestler at Allegheny College, I developed discipline and resilience while graduating magna cum laude in economics and political science. I worked my way through law school in steel mills and other jobs, giving me real-world perspective. Over a 30-year legal career, I’ve led high-stakes cases and teams across major industries. As chairman of the Allegheny County Republican Party, I’ve managed a large, complex organization. All of these have prepared me to lead and govern on day one. The lieutenant governor's duties include chairing the Board of Pardons, which determines when or if the state should grant clemency to people convicted of crimes. Under what conditions do you see yourself, if ever, pardoning someone who comes before you? I have talked with Attorney General Dave Sunday on this important topic. Each case is different. The background/story of the applicant and the details surrounding his/her crime needs to be evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Having said that, I do believe that those engaged in violent crimes against other humans will have a higher bar to climb to achieve a successful application. In addition to duties required by law, a lieutenant governor can take on initiatives at the governor's direction. Lieutenant governors have, for example, advocated for community violence-prevention efforts and gathered public input on the legalization of marijuana. What cause would you most like to pursue if given an opportunity to do so? When Stacy Garrity asked me to join her ticket, she promised we'd be a true governing team. I'm focused on two priorities. First, energy: Pennsylvania holds natural gas reserves that rival Saudi Arabia's oil, yet this potential is virtually untapped. Partnering with the private sector to responsibly develop these resources can generate billions in revenue, create hundreds of thousands of jobs, and cut taxes for every Pennsylvanian. Second, government reform: After 12 years of Democratic rule, Pennsylvania has over 160,000 regulatory restrictions strangling small businesses. I will root out waste, fraud, and abuse, and find more ways to make state government more efficient and effective for the Commonwealth’s citizens. Pennsylvania voters choose lieutenant governors in a separate standalone race, so a gubernatorial candidate could end up with a running mate who doesn't share the same agenda. Do you think this position should be elected independently, and why or why not? I do not think that the governor and lieutenant governor should be elected independently in the General Election. I would support looking into whether it makes sense for the voters and taxpayers to allow the candidates for governor and their respective parties to choose the lieutenant governor candidates like the way Vice-Presidential candidates are chosen. Ventre has run unsuccessfully for governor and Westmoreland County commissioner, but he says he hopes to draw on grassroots support to make the November ballot this time. And he doesn’t like endorsements, having lost his commissioners’ race to a party-backed incumbent, he said. “The primary is for the people to choose their candidate,” he told WESA. “What are you afraid of?” Ventre has a college degree in criminal justice and was head of regional security at UPS, which he says gave him executive experience and a background in crisis management. He’s also a prolific author of fiction books that concern UFO sightings, and he says creativity is needed to make state government efficient. Education: A.A., criminal justice, SUNY of Farmingdale; unfinished degree, C.W. Post Campus of Long Island University Related experience: UPS crisis management, security director and executive experience, lived or worked in several U.S. states and Europe offering a broader perspective, Ventre says Supporters/endorsements: Trump-aligned “Patriot groups” and the pro-life groups LifePAC and Pennsylvania Pro-Life Federation PAC Total raised: No contributions reported, but $215.98 in in-kind contributions.Total spent: $2,729.12, paid directly by the candidate Why do you think you will be an effective running mate in terms of helping your party's candidate for governor win this November? I am a “patriot candidate,” similar to [GOP 2022 gubernatorial nominee Doug] Mastriano but different in that I would’ve never voted for Act 77 [a 2019 election reform bill that made vote-by-mail easier]. I am similar to Trump, having grown up in NYC. I bring the patriot vote whereas endorsements bring committee help — a huge difference. My experience for UPS brings a results-oriented expertise similar to Stacy Garrity. My resume matches exactly with the duties of lieutenant governor: oversee the Senate, parole board, and handle crises. I ran public affairs with a team of 22 executives who would meet with senators and congresspeople in Pennsylvania, West Virginia and Virginia. If you are part of a winning gubernatorial ticket this fall, there is a chance that you may have to ascend to the post of governor should your gubernatorial nominee have to vacate it. What in your background prepares you for that responsibility? I have 26 years of executive experience as a multistate security, public affairs, crisis management, and claims director. My opponent is an energy attorney and is qualified for secretary of energy for PA. I have a 27-point plan to improve PA listed on my website. The governor carries less responsibility and demands day-to-day than when I was working for UPS in charge of Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Virginia for the responsibilities mentioned over my final 15 years of employment. I also lived and worked in New York, Oklahoma, and New Jersey with the same duties. I am also very creative and have published 14 books [novels]. The lieutenant governor's duties include chairing the Board of Pardons, which determines when or if the state should grant clemency to people convicted of crimes. Under what conditions do you see yourself, if ever, pardoning someone who comes before you? I have a degree in criminal justice and spent 26 years at UPS as their state security director in six states. I plan to review each case on death row with an emphasis on the evidence. There can be no hearsay on death row. The evidence must be 100 percent, such as video or DNA. Pardons or life in prison as opposed to death row will depend on the strength of the evidence. Justice vs. forgiveness is totally different. I am open-minded to the evidence presented or new evidence like DNA. In addition to duties required by law, a lieutenant governor can take on initiatives at the governor's direction. Lieutenant governors have, for example, advocated for community violence-prevention efforts and gathered public input on the legalization of marijuana. What cause would you most like to pursue if given an opportunity to do so? We need a DOSE, or Department of State Efficiency. We cannot keep spending 7 percent more than we take in. This also includes overspending on education, which makes up 38 percent of the budget but whose results are ranked at 39th nationally. We spend $25,000 per student, which ranks among the top 5-7 highest in the country. I also believe through efficiency, we can eliminate the fuel tax, freeze property taxes for seniors and eliminate property taxes for disabled vets. At UPS, our motto was, “We run the tightest ship in the shipping business.” PA needs to run more like a business and downsize and outsource. Pennsylvania voters choose lieutenant governors in a separate standalone race, so a gubernatorial candidate could end up with a running mate who doesn't share the same agenda. Do you think this position should be elected independently, and why or why not? Absolutely should NOT be appointed. We don’t need yes [men]. I already have some different views than Stacy, and that is healthy, and I plan to influence her to change her plans and objectives. My plan makes Pennsylvania efficient as the gateway to the northeast. We should be sucking jobs and corporate HQs from the more expensive northeast. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: US House 17th District Republican primary: Tony Guy v. Jesse James Vodvarka - 90.5 WESA
content: What's at stake: The Republican primary in the 17th District is, as one of the candidates put it, “a fight between Jesse James and the sheriff”: Jesse James Vodvarka, who helps to run his family’s spring- and wireform-manufacturing business, is facing Beaver County Sheriff Tony Guy in a bid to take on Democratic incumbent Congressman Chris Deluizio this fall. Deluzio was first elected in 2022, and he’s emerged as a sharp critic of President Donald Trump’s second administration, questioning Trump’s immigration priorities and drawing the White House’s ire for taking part in a video urging American servicepeople not to follow illegal orders. The 17th District is made up of Beaver County and many of the Allegheny County suburbs around Pittsburgh. Pundits say Democrats have the advantage here, and so far, there’s been little sign of Republican groups making an investment in the effort to topple Deluzio. But both Guy and Vodvarka appear to have pinned their hopes on beating the odds with an “America First” message in the fall. Members of the U.S. House draft, debate, and vote on legislation, including the federal budget. They also sit on committees with jurisdiction over specific policy areas. The House also initiates efforts to impeach federal officials, though the Senate must confirm that judgment before the official can be removed. House members serve two-year terms and earn $174,000 per year. Beaver County’s sheriff has long been a vocal supporter of MAGA policies — particularly on immigration — and he’s been involved in federal campaigning before, having appeared at Trump rallies and starred in ads attacking former U.S. Sen. Bob Casey. He’s running his own campaign for Congress this time, but with a familiar message of supporting “an America First Agenda” while seeking to “stop the America-hating left in their tracks.” Guy’s campaign did not respond to the WESA questionnaire. Related experience: None provided, but Guy was previously a member of the Pennsylvania State Police What is the most important thing the federal government could do for your district, and how would you make it happen if you were elected to Congress? Candidate did not return questionnaire by deadline. Polling consistently shows many voters are worried about the cost of living, especially when it comes to essentials such as housing and food. How do you think Congress should address these concerns? Candidate did not return questionnaire by deadline. There have been bipartisan concerns through the years that Congress has ceded much of its role as a check on the executive branch, particularly on military action or policy concerns such as tariffs. How do you think Congress is doing in this respect, and in which areas, if any, would you push for it to be more assertive? Candidate did not return questionnaire by deadline. Artificial intelligence is said to be a game-changing technology, and the construction of data centers themselves can generate jobs. But there are concerns that the centers may drive up utility costs and disrupt communities, and that AI could cause massive economic and other disruptions. What do you think federal policy toward AI should be? Candidate did not return questionnaire by deadline. President Trump has sought to overhaul the conduct of elections, with proposals that have included voter ID requirements that go beyond presenting a driver’s license and limiting access to mail-in voting. How confident are you in our election processes now, and which of these proposals — or any other proposed changes — do you support? Candidate did not return questionnaire by deadline. Vodvarka helps to run a family spring-manufacturing business, and he is particularly outspoken on U.S. trade and manufacturing policy. A key reason for running, he says, is to support the economic agenda of President Donald Trump, who Vodvarka says is “the first president of my lifetime who wasn’t a globalist.” He also backs term limits and reforms to limit government spending. Vodvarka has not previously held elected office. He has run for this seat previously but was removed from the ballot. That continued a family tradition started by his father, whose efforts to get on the ballot for U.S. Senator have met with mixed results and provided the basis for a 2016 state Supreme Court case addressing petition-signature requirements. Education: Associate degree from Le Cordon Bleu Culinary Academy Current occupation: Manufacturer/manager of a small family-owned manufacturing business Total raised: None reportedTotal spent: None reported What is the most important thing the federal government could do for your district, and how would you make it happen if you were elected to Congress? Encourage the export of liquified natural gas and growth of the industry. This sector of the economy could single-handedly lead the growth of wealth in our region for generations to come. Polling consistently shows many voters are worried about the cost of living, especially when it comes to essentials such as housing and food. How do you think Congress should address these concerns? The greatest issue that affects our spending is the interest that we pay on the national debt. At roughly $1 trillion a year, this puts a great financial burden on the tax payer yet the congress continues to add to it by not balancing the budget. Reducing the interest rates will help this as well as home building, resolving the Iran conflict will end the current spike in oil prices, but paying down the debt and balancing the budget will give American’s long-term financial security. There have been bipartisan concerns through the years that Congress has ceded much of its role as a check on the executive branch, particularly on military action or policy concerns such as tariffs. How do you think Congress is doing in this respect, and in which areas, if any, would you push for it to be more assertive? In my opinion, Congress has done very little on many grounds especially on passing an actual budget. It seems like there’s a continuing resolution or even current funding for TSA not being addressed. If those functions aren’t being addressed, how can they be expected to address anything else? Artificial intelligence is said to be a game-changing technology, and the construction of data centers themselves can generate jobs. But there are concerns that the centers may drive up utility costs and disrupt communities, and that AI could cause massive economic and other disruptions. What do you think federal policy toward AI should be? This is an area that I’m still learning about from both inside and outside the district and forming an opinion. President Trump has sought to overhaul the conduct of elections, with proposals that have included voter ID requirements that go beyond presenting a driver’s license and limiting access to mail-in voting. How confident are you in our election processes now, and which of these proposals — or any other proposed changes — do you support? B) Eliminate mail-in ballots [except for] those in the military or with medical conditions. The way mail-in ballots are being handled now makes it too easy to cheat! C) Would be supportive on the state level to reform ballot-access laws in PA. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: State Senate 42nd District Democratic primary: Wayne Fontana v. Paul Steenkiste - 90.5 WESA
content: What’s at stake: No matter the outcome of his dark-horse candidacy in the May Democratic primary, Paul Steenkiste’s challenge to Wayne Fontana will make history: The incumbent has not had a single challenger in either the primary or the general election since he first won the seat in 2005. Fontana has long been an ally to Democratic establishment leaders that include Pittsburgh Mayor Corey O’Connor and former Allegheny County Executive Rich Fitzgerald; Steenkiste is a first-time candidate who says the district — and the party as a whole — need change. The solidly blue district includes much of Pittsburgh and some 17 suburbs to the south and west of the city, ranging from Mt. Lebanon to Emsworth. The race has drawn no Republican entrant, which means district voters may be able to reset the clock on their series of uncontested races this fall. Pennsylvania’s 50 state senators each represent a district of roughly 260,000 voters. Along with the state House, the Senate reviews, revises, and ultimately passes the state budget, and the tax rates on which it is based. The body also drafts and passes legislation concerning everything from election and environmental rules to firearms laws, reproductive rights, and criminal penalties. Senators are also individually appointed to committees that specialize in various policy areas and have the power to reshape, stymie, or advance legislation. The Senate also has the power to confirm or deny many gubernatorial nominations to state commissions and other offices. Republicans have maintained solid control of the upper chamber for nearly a quarter-century. State senators serve four-year terms. Their salary is adjusted for inflation each year; they will be paid $113,591.40 in 2026 Fontana has been a fixture of Pittsburgh politics for a quarter-century as a member of Allegheny County Council and a state senator. There had been talk that he might retire instead of seeking reelection, but Fontana has said he was reinvigorated by Corey O’Connor’s win in Pittsburgh’s mayoral election last year. In Harrisburg, he’s been a reliable Democratic vote. He touts his success at bringing state dollars back home to the district (though Fontana, who once worked in real estate, has so far been frustrated seeking to establish a statewide policy on property reassessments rather than having Allegheny County be intermittently reassessed by court order). He is the top Democrat on the Senate’s Law and Justice committee. In addition, he chairs the Pennsylvania Higher Education Assistance Agency, which finances student loans, as well as the local agency that oversees Pittsburgh’s major sports facilities and convention center. Related experience: Boilermaker, Teamster, Realtor, Vice chair of Allegheny County Democratic Committee, 19th Ward Committeeperson, Allegheny County Council member, state senator, 2005-present Supporters/endorsements: Allegheny County Democratic Committee, Young Democrats of Allegheny County, Char Valley Democratic Committee, Steel City Stonewall Democrats, Planned Parenthood, Allegheny-Fayette Central Labor Council, AFL-CIO, Laborers 1058, IBEW Local 5, Steamfitters Local 449, Mayor Corey O'Connor, Allegheny County Executive Sara Innamorato Total raised: $21,900 in 2026, plus $322,657.91 on hand at the end of 2025.Total spent: $39,243.42 in 2026 What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? It has been 17 years since our minimum-wage workers have received a raise from $7.25/hr. And our dysfunctional property assessment system is a national outlier — the only state in the country that does not reassess on a scheduled cycle. And the Republicans' refusal to help us address the gun-violence epidemic is shameful. But Harrisburg's inability to address skill games is most frustrating. Companies exploit a legal loophole to flood our communities with unregulated machines. There is no one ensuring that the machines are fair, that children are not using them, or that shops and social clubs are receiving their money owed. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? There is not one policy that will adequately address housing affordability. Instead, we need to provide local governments and builders with a toolbox of policies and incentives. My experience serving on Habitat for Humanity and the Land Bank led me to introduce legislation that became Act 4 of 2023 to streamline the sheriff sale process in Allegheny County, providing our land bank with an expedited timeline to rehabilitate properties. In Harrisburg, I have worked to bring over $16 million of state investment to address housing affordability through the PA Housing Finance Authority. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? There is no doubt that the historic investment the state has made in public education over the past several state budgets is having a positive effect on our students. But state funding is only one of three sources of funding for public schools. With the Trump administration currently dismantling the U.S. Department of Education, there will be additional financial stress on the state and local school districts to provide adequate education funding. Last year, we in the General Assembly were able to enact cyber-charter school reporting improvements that are saving local districts millions. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? I believe that Pittsburgh and Western Pennsylvania can become an international hub for AI innovation. But leadership at all levels of government, private industry, and community organizations must work to ensure that no one in our community is left bearing the environmental brunt of this industry. We've seen that play out throughout our history. I support the governor's proposals for purposeful and thoughtful data center permitting and siting standards. However, I do not support state-level preemption of municipal planning efforts. Communities must be able to make those decisions themselves without political interference from Harrisburg. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? That discontent is real. I believe much of that has seeped in from the chaos and disruption of our national politics. But voters have a right to be upset when government isn't working for them. At the state level, we can deliver to Pennsylvanians of all political leanings by ensuring that government is efficient and responsive. People are rightfully frustrated paying a high gas tax when the bridge they travel collapses. Democrats win when we listen to the needs of citizens and act — better schools, safer neighborhoods, and more job opportunities. First-time candidate Paul Steenkiste candidly acknowledges that at present, “people haven’t heard of me — I’m just a guy.” But he says the 42nd District and the Senate itself could use some shaking up. A software engineer, Steenkiste says the Senate lacks the expertise needed to reckon with the challenges posed by data centers, artificial intelligence, and other technologies. And he cites last year’s months-overdue state budget as recent evidence that “inaction is the name of the game on a lot of issues” in Harrisburg. Republicans control the Senate and much of the legislative calendar, but Steenkiste says Democrats “have to ask ourselves why that is,” when they’ve won control of the House and a number of statewide races in recent years. “It’s a failure to project a vision and craft a program that resonates with voters,” he said. Education: Bachelor’s degree in math, Stanford University; master’s degree in computer science, Stanford University Related experience: Leadership roles in software companies of up to 40 employees. Total raised: Not yet availableTotal spent: Not yet available What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? Virtually none of the concerns of our communities have been met by action in Harrisburg. Pittsburgh Regional Transit is headed towards a financial cliff because our state leaders haven’t taken action to fund public transit since 2013. Extending the sales tax to ridesharing companies like Uber and Lyft (which are currently exempt) would raise well over $200 million. Many Pennsylvanians are still making $7.25 per hour and lack basic rights that workers in other states enjoy. We must raise the minimum wage to $15 with annual adjustments for inflation, and we must bolster collective bargaining rights, including prohibiting employers from stripping health care coverage from families during a work stoppage. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? Our state leaders’ failure to respond to the affordable housing crisis is pushing people out of their homes and the communities they grew up in. We must finally implement the Home Preservation Grant Program, which would help struggling homeowners make much-needed repairs and buttress our aging housing stock. Pennsylvania must provide more support for our region’s land banks, which suffer from myopic inefficiencies in state law. Finally, our leaders must fight back against the out-of-state mega-corporations that are monopolizing housing units across our communities. For example, the state should partner with municipalities to enable stricter code enforcement against the violations of these corporate slumlords. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? The marginal increase in state spending does not nearly cover the unconstitutional funding gap that the Commonwealth Court ordered the legislature to fix. For example, the gap for Sto-Rox was measured at over $23 million per year, but so far the state has provided less than $2.6 million per year. Our current state leaders have no intention of ever fully closing the gap. It is disgusting. Instead, our state leaders allocated $700 million last year to private and religious schools, a majority of which openly discriminate against disabled and LGBTQ+ students. As state senator, I will fight to end state-sponsored discrimination and redirect public dollars back to public schools. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? Data centers are already costing Pennsylvanians $2 billion per year through our electric bills. The politicians in Harrisburg have done nothing to regulate them, blaming partisan gridlock. That gridlock didn’t stop them from passing bipartisan legislation to give hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars to data center corporations every year. We must require data centers to pay their fair share, including paying for the upgrades to our grid infrastructure they are necessitating. Without specific cost-allocation legislation, those costs are being spread across everyone’s electric bills. We must also equip municipalities to protect their water supply by updating the PA Municipalities Planning Code for the first time in 40 years. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? People are angry and disillusioned with both parties because the elected leaders of both parties act the exact same: they prioritize corporate profit over their own constituents. If Democrats are to regain the trust of voters, they must stop accepting gifts and campaign donations from lobbyists, and then turning around and voting for whatever bill benefits the corporations that hired those lobbyists. We must pass legislation prohibiting officials from accepting gifts, which 47 states have already done. Only when Democrats fight back against corruption, instead of gleefully taking part in the bacchanalia of greed, will working people see themselves and their values reflected in our party. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: State House 45th District Democratic primary: Brittany Bloam v. Pat Catena - 90.5 WESA
content: What’s at stake: State Rep. Antia Kulik’s decision to retire after a decade made the 45th the only open House seat in Allegheny County, and the race to replace her may be the most competitive on the Democratic primary ballot. At one point, four Democrats were seeking the nomination. Two have withdrawn, leaving Allegheny County Council President Pat Catena fending off a spirited challenge from political newcomer Brittany Bloam. The district, which is made up of suburban communities to the west and southwest of Pittsburgh, skews somewhat more conservative than other House districts in the area, making it a potential battleground for Republicans. The GOP’s James Julius is unopposed this spring and is set to face whichever champion the Democrats select. Allegheny County Democratic Committee endorses Catena, Altemara and Ferderber in state House races (Chris Potter, WESA) Bloam picks up Kulik endorsement in 45th Pa. House District race (Chris Potter, WESA) There are 203 members of the state House, each of whom represents a district of roughly 64,000 people. Along with the state Senate, the House reviews, negotiates on, and passes the state budget — and the tax rates on which it is based. Its members also draft legislation on topics that range from election and environmental rules to firearms laws, reproductive rights, and criminal penalties. House members are also appointed to committees that specialize in various policy areas and have the power to reshape, stymie, or advance legislation. Democrats have held a narrow majority of House seats since 2023. State House members serve two-year terms. Their salary is adjusted for inflation each year; they will be paid $113,591.40 in 2026. Bloam is a first-time candidate and the last entrant to join the race in HD-45. But the land-use attorney has quickly emerged as a serious contender in the race, having secured the backing of the retiring incumbent, Anita Kulik, even as Pat Catena draws on support from other established Democrats. A native of Elk County, Bloam and her family live in the house where her husband grew up, and she stresses her own experiences as a working parent. Further reading: Robinson lawyer Brittany Bloam joins race to replace Rep. Kulik in Pa. House District 45 (Chris Potter, WESA) Education: B.A., Gettysburg College; J.D. University of Pittsburgh School of Law Related experience: “I’m a working mom, lawyer, and lifelong advocate for strong communities. … I worked my way through school, and my 15-year legal career reflects a deep respect for the rule of law, public institutions, and the real-world consequences of policy decisions.” Supporters/endorsements: Bloam is backed by retiring state Rep. Anita Kulik and Coraopolis Mayor Michael Dixon. Union supporters include SEIU, IBEW Local 5, IUPAT DC 57; other endorsers include reproductive rights advocacy groups Planned Parenthood Pennsylvania Advocates and Emily’s List, as well as the Working Families Party and Run for Something. Total raised: Not yet availableTotal spent: Not yet available What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? Families across our communities are feeling the pressure of rising costs, and they deserve leadership focused on real relief. I am committed to policies that make our lives more affordable, without tax increases. We must be responsible with public tax dollars by setting clear priorities to address the cost of living, and ensuring the government works efficiently for the people it serves. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? As a land-use attorney, I am well-versed in the development process and the challenges to getting projects off the ground. I support streamlining and modernizing the Commonwealth’s patchwork of approval requirements to make the process more efficient, while also maintaining a commitment to making housing attainable for all. One strategy is to encourage municipalities to allow accessory dwelling units (ADUs) in their communities. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? As a public school parent, I am committed to being a strong advocate for increased state funding for our school districts. We need to ensure that all students receive equal opportunities, regardless of their ZIP codes. We also need to ensure that the Commonwealth meets its obligations so our school districts are not forced to raise property taxes, which further exacerbates the affordability crisis that our families and senior citizens are facing. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? The biggest concern with data centers are the environmental effects and impacts on already-rising costs of energy. I support HB 1834, sponsored by Representative Matzie, which would regulate the growth of data centers and protect the power grid while also working to prevent energy cost hikes to Pennsylvanians. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? Discontent with the current political system is part of the reason I entered this race. It is critical that our government reflects the lived experiences of our citizens, and those voices are included in the decisions being made in Harrisburg — not just career politicians. It is critical that elected officials meet voters where they are and commit to making government a vehicle of positivity in our communities. That starts at the local level with tangible state investments in things like schools, infrastructure, and health care. Catena entered the 45th District race last year as the putative frontrunner, thanks to local roots and long experience in local government. A native of Carnegie with experience in borough government there, he now leads Allegheny County Council as its president during a period in which it has taken a more proactive role in shaping county policy. He’s been backed by a number of local Democrats, though the endorsement of outgoing House member Anita Kulik went to Bloam. Further reading: Allegheny County Council President Catena launches bid to replace Kulik in state House (Chris Potter, Julia Zenkevich, WESA) Education: B.A., political science and communications, with certificate in Public Service, University of Pittsburgh. Related experience: I am a third-generation lifelong resident of Carnegie. I have been a member of Allegheny County Council since 2017 and have served as its president since 2020. Prior to that, I was on Carnegie Borough Council and served as their president. Supporters/endorsements: Catena has been endorsed by the Allegheny County Democratic Committee and other notable Democrats, including the Chartiers Valley Democratic Chairs Organization, state Sen. Wayne Fontana, state Rep. Dan Deasy, and Allegheny County Sheriff Kevin Kraus. He touts support from the union representing Pittsburgh Regional Transit employees and a number of building trades organizations, including Steamfitters Local 448 and Plumbers Local 27. Total raised: None reportedTotal spent: None reported (A committee Catena used to support his 2025 bid for county council ended the year with $59,998.89 on hand and a debt of $56,000 to Catena himself for money loaned to the committee. The money could be transferred to a state House campaign.) What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? The 45th District needs fair school funding, strong economic development, and modern infrastructure, including real solutions to flooding along the Route 50 corridor and reliable funding for mass transit. As President of Allegheny County Council, I increased funding for CCAC, supported workforce development programs, and championed the volunteer firefighter tax credit. I led downtown revitalization, blight remediation, code enforcement, and flood mitigation efforts, while improving communication and transparency with residents. I currently serve on a legislative body, where I’ve focused on delivering results, transparency, and personally making responsible fiscal decisions. If elected to Harrisburg, I’ll ensure schools, public safety systems, local economies — and transit systems — have the resources they need to thrive. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? Housing costs are rising faster than wages in our district. I support zoning reforms for responsible infill housing, incentives for mixed-income development, and first-time homebuyer programs. In Carnegie, I worked to remediate blight, attract responsible developers and landlords, and expand affordable housing, strengthening neighborhoods and local tax bases. At the state level, I will apply these lessons to increase housing supply, preserve community character, and ensure working families have access to safe, affordable homes. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? State funding increases have helped schools retain teachers, expand programs, and better support students with special needs. As County Council President, I prioritized community college funding and workforce training programs, creating affordable pathways to careers. In Carnegie, I partnered with schools to enhance youth programs, strengthen school safety through collaboration with the Carnegie Police Department, and give students a voice in local government by establishing a Junior Council program — of which current Mayor Sam Bingham is a graduate. These experiences guide my commitment to invest in education, support students, and build strong pathways from the classroom to careers. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? Residents in the 45th District are raising concerns about energy demand, environmental impact, and whether large-scale data centers deliver real local benefits.
