There are also growing questions about how artificial intelligence is used in schools and workplaces, particularly around privacy, transparency, and job impacts. I support clear state standards for data centers, including energy efficiency, responsible locations, and coordination with local infrastructure. For AI, I support guardrails that ensure transparency, protect personal data, and prevent misuse, especially in education and public services. We should encourage innovation while making sure it benefits communities and protects residents. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? Voters are frustrated because they don’t see results on issues that impact their daily lives: cost of living, schools, housing, and public safety. We rebuild trust by focusing on results: investing in public education, workforce development, affordable housing, and infrastructure, while protecting workers’ rights and strengthening communities — and living within our means. As President of Allegheny County Council, I have worked to increase transparency, make responsible fiscal decisions, and deliver results. My record in Carnegie – addressing blight, responding to severe weather and flooding, improving critical infrastructure, workforce programs, and ensuring government lives within its means — shows I know how to govern. In Harrisburg, I will bring that same results-driven experience. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: State House 28th District Democratic primary: Robert Bertha v. Jeremy Ferderber - 90.5 WESA
content: What’s at stake: Democrats have made inroads in Pittsburgh’s northern suburbs, but the prosperous bedroom communities of District 28 — which sprawls across Allegheny County’s northern tier — have remained in Republican hands. The district includes Bradford Woods borough and Hampton, Pine, Richland, and West Deer townships. It’s currently represented by first-term Republican Jeremy Shaffer, and the GOP has a voter-registration edge there of about 10%. Two Democrats are competing to challenge Shaffer’s first reelection bid. While they take broadly similar positions on the issues and stress their connections to the area, they bring different backgrounds to the campaign. There are 203 members of the state House, each of whom represents a district of roughly 64,000 people. Along with the state Senate, the House reviews, negotiates on, and passes the state budget — and the tax rates on which it is based. Its members also draft legislation on topics that range from election and environmental rules to firearms laws, reproductive rights, and criminal penalties. House members are also appointed to committees that specialize in various policy areas and have the power to reshape, stymie, or advance legislation. Democrats have held a narrow majority of House seats since 2023. State House members serve two-year terms. Their salary is adjusted for inflation each year; they will be paid $113,591.40 in 2026. A West Deer native, Bertha says he knows how tumultuous politics can be: As a former federal government employee working in international affairs, his job was a casualty of the chaotic, Elon Musk-led “Department of Government Efficiency.” Bertha ran unsuccessfully for state House in 2018, but he’s running this time in part because “Harrisburg is where we can stop the damage being done at the federal level.” Doing so means shoring up investments in health care and education, and providing tax relief for working-class families, he says. And even in the leafy suburbs of the 28th District, Bertha says affordability is a concern, as is the need for enhanced vocational and job-training options outside of college. Education: Bachelor’s degree from Point Park University; master’s degree from George Washington University Current occupation: Communications and programs manager at Northside/North Shore Chamber of Commerce Related experience: Born and raised in West Deer Township, volunteer firefighter (2013-2019). Federal employee in international affairs and diplomatic relations (2021-2025). Supporters/endorsements: Endorsed by Iron Workers Union Local 3 Total raised: Not yet availableTotal spent: Not yet available What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? One of the biggest concerns I hear is the rising cost of living, especially health care. Families are struggling, and Harrisburg must do more to lower costs and expand access to affordable, quality care. I also hear a strong need for more vocational and career pathways in our schools. Success shouldn’t be limited to four-year degrees, we should better support students entering trades, union apprenticeships, or starting their own businesses. If elected, I will work to lower everyday costs and invest in career and technical education so residents can build stable, well-paying futures. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? Pennsylvania’s housing shortage is driven in large part by the lack of starter homes. Too many families are being priced out because we’re not building the kinds of homes first-time buyers can afford. I believe we need to work collaboratively with developers and local communities to encourage the construction of smaller, more affordable homes. That includes updating zoning, streamlining permitting, and offering targeted incentives for projects that prioritize workforce and starter housing. We should also support programs that help first-time buyers with down payments and closing costs. Housing is the foundation of stable communities, and we need practical solutions to ensure more Pennsylvanians can afford a place to call home. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? Increased education funding is an important step toward addressing long-standing inequities, and I believe it is beginning to make a positive impact. Schools in our district are better positioned to support students, invest in resources, and address gaps that have existed for too long. That said, funding alone isn’t enough. We need to ensure those dollars are used effectively. I support continued investment in public education, with a focus on reducing class sizes, expanding mental health resources, and strengthening career and technical education programs. We should also ensure funding is distributed fairly and predictably so every student, regardless of their ZIP code, has access to a high-quality education. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? I have concerns about the rapid expansion of data centers, particularly around the strain they place on local infrastructure and the potential for higher electricity and water rates. We need to ensure they are not driving up utility costs for residents or putting additional pressure on our natural resources. The state should implement clear regulations requiring transparency around energy and water usage, as well as safeguards to protect consumers from rate increases tied to large-scale developments. We should also consider impact fees or targeted levies to ensure centers contribute to the infrastructure and resources they rely on. Growth in this sector must be balanced with protecting residents and maintaining affordability. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? I think a big part of the growing discontent is the increasing polarization people experience. Many voters feel that politics has become more about division than solving problems. Social media has played a role in that, as platforms often amplify the most extreme and inflammatory content, instead of encouraging constructive dialogue. At the state level, I believe we can start to rebuild trust by focusing on results, lowering costs, improving schools, and strengthening communities. We also need to prioritize transparency, accountability, and open communication so people feel heard and represented. Ultimately, voters want leaders who are focused on getting things done, and that’s the approach I will bring. Ferderber jokes that his general election message could be, “I’m the Jeremy you need, not the Jeremy you know.” He says he got into the race both to help make the state a better place for his children and the rest of the community, and because “I was sick and tired of being sick and tired and not being able to do anything about it.” He grew up on a family-owned organic farm in Butler County, and he now works at a natural-foods distributor, campaigning as a "certified organic homegrown community member.” Ferderber’s campaign pledges “level-headed discourse” and more civility in politics as the national political landscape” seems to be crumbling around us.” Current occupation: Vice president at Frankferd Farms Foods Inc. Related experience: Parent of two children (ages 15 and 11), soccer coach, school volunteer, book review committee member. Supporters/endorsements: Young Democrats of Allegheny County (recommended candidate), Allegheny County Democratic Committee (endorsed candidate), endorsed by pro-science advocacy group 314 Action. Total raised: Not yet availableTotal spent: Not yet available What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? While many challenges in my district reflect broader national issues, some can be addressed at the state level. Affordability remains a top concern, with high gas taxes and rising food costs impacting families. Labor shortages, partly due to immigration enforcement, are also affecting prices. Additionally, while some areas in my district are growing and prospering, others face decline and impending food insecurity. Strategic reinvestment of tax dollars into local infrastructure can support both economic development and residential stability, helping our community thrive. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? I grew up just north of here, so I’ve seen firsthand how our communities have changed over time. While District 28 has grown, affordable housing both locally and statewide remains a real concern. I believe we should focus on revitalizing areas with existing infrastructure as much as we focus on expanding into untouched land. Growing up in a rural area around generations-old farmland that became subdivisions, I understand the balance we need. We can protect our environment while streamlining permitting and encouraging redevelopment. That approach helps increase affordable housing and strengthens our local economy without losing what makes this area special. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? My district is home to several highly regarded school districts, and my own personal experience as a father of two school-aged children has been overwhelmingly positive. However, PA’s educational system remains underfunded while our per-student cost is among the highest in the nation. I would support any legislation that can help to address both of these issues, while placing the educational benefits of all students first. Updating state funding based on current data, increasing state contributions closer to the national average, and reassessing charter (including cyber) regulations to include more financial, ethical, and private interest transparency would be positive first steps to take. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? I believe it would be naive to think the AI boom is not happening, or that it can be stopped at this point, although the environmentalist in me wishes we could slow it down. I also think that the demand for data centers (at least as we currently know them) will only increase until the next wave of technological advancement. However, we need to learn from previous industrial booms that came at a significant environmental cost, and be proactive rather than reactive with responsible development that creates jobs while also conserving our precious natural resources, balancing innovation with conservation. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? As a non-politician and first-time candidate, I completely understand this discontent with both parties. Both have their faults, but one carries the lion’s share of responsibility for our current issues. There’s no magic wand, but voters have to feel like they are voting for someone they can relate to. My party needs to show it is willing to stand tall in protecting the rights that we are losing at the national level, so that our daughters in the commonwealth can grow up with the same freedoms their mothers and grandmothers had. Accountability on the right needs to be met with sensible and pragmatic legislation from the left. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: Vote for Section V's Girls Athlete of the Week for April 13-19 presented by Faber Builders - Democrat and Chronicle
content: It's time to take a look at the standout performers from Section V. Your vote will determine who will be the Faber Builders Girls Sports Athlete of the Week for April 13-19. The players' credentials are listed above the poll — which will be live until 8 p.m. Wednesday. The winner will be announced on our Instagram page Thursday morning. The nominees are chosen by our sports reporters. If you would like to call attention to an athlete's performance to be considered for the nomination, please email sports@democratandchronicle.com by 7 p.m. each Sunday. All athletes who play on Section V girls teams are eligible to be nominated. The poll can be found at the bottom of the article. Now, let's meet the candidates. Fairport senior is the one to beat on the Section V Girls Outdoor Track and Field 100-meter dash season leaderboard after she ran a winning time of 12.32 seconds during the Webster Invitational. Avon softball pitcher was near-perfect in two outings. She pitched a complete game with 16 strikeouts and only two hits allowed in a 9-2 win against York-Pavilion, to pair with a 4-for-4 showing at the plate. Crye then had 15 strikeouts and one run against in 6 1/3 innings against LeRoy. Pittsford Mendon junior jumped 11 feet to win the pole vault event at the Webster Invitational. Aquinas girls lacrosse player tallied 11 goals, five assists and 14 draw controls across two wins for the Lil Irish. Bloomfield softball player hit for the cycle in a win against Marcus Whitman, finishing 4-for-5 with seven RBIs. Aquinas senior finished with 3 touchdown passes, 2 touchdown runs, an interception for a touchdown and a punt return for a touchdown during two wins for the school's flag football team. Waterloo softball player went 3-for-4 with two runs, a steal and a triple in the Tigers’ win against South Seneca/Romulus. With her three-bagger, Panek became the state’s all-time leader in career triples with 32. Canandaigua girls lacrosse player had the game-winner in overtime of the Gray Wolves’ victory against defending state champion Penfield. Sheridan finished the game with three goals and two assists. Oakfield-Alabama softball player was MVP of the Barker tournament after reaching base safely in eight out of nine at-bats. In the semifinal against league opponent Elba, Thompson went 3-for-3 with three RBIs. LeRoy softball player had a solo home run, which served as the only run of the game in a 1-0 victory against Avon.

title: Coming to Xbox Game Pass: Kiln, Aphelion, Final Fantasy V, and More - Xbox Wire
content: What do Chocobos, space, vampires, and sentient pottery have in common? If it’s anything more than “themes of games you can play soon” we’d really like to know what kind of weird list that is. Also, don’t forget to tune in to the ID@Xbox and IGN showcase to see spotlights on a some upcoming games (including some coming day one to Game Pass like There Are No Ghosts at the Grand, Aphelion, and Mistfall Hunter)! Go grab your keyboard and controller and let’s get to the games. Little Rocket Lab (Cloud, Console, and PC) – April 21Now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass Little Rocket Lab is a cozy, machine building RPG. You play as Morgan, an aspiring engineer, who heads home to St. Ambroise to help complete their family’s dream-building a rocket. Help the locals with new machines, be creative, and become the ultimate engineer! That rocket isn’t going to build itself. Sopa: Tale of the Stolen Potato (Cloud, Console, Handheld, and PC) – April 21Now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass Miho steps into the pantry to fetch a potato for his grandma and falls into a magical world full of wonders. Meet quirky characters, venture through the depths of South America, and learn the importance of the things we pass along in this unforgettable emotional narrative adventure game. Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 21Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one on Xbox Game Pass! From the creators of Vampire Survivors, Vampire Crawlers turns the thrill of Survivors into a hyper turn‑based, rogue-lite card‑driven blobber. Build busted decks, explore familiar dungeons, and unleash world‑ending combos. Either take your time and be tactical or play turns as fast as you humanly can: The outcome is always accurate! Kiln (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 23Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass!Kiln is a pottery power‑fantasy celebrating both creativity and destruction — the pleasure of making beautiful things, then smashing them to smithereens. Create your pot, grab your friends, and throw down in online arenas, where the ceramic creations you sculpt become the bodies you bring into battle. Aphelion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 28Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Aphelion is a third-person sci-fi adventure developed by Don’t Nod (Jusant, Lost Records: Bloom & Rage). After crash-landing on a frozen planet, astronaut Ariane must traverse harsh terrain and shifting realities to save her injured partner, Thomas. Players control both characters in a story rooted in real-world science, created in partnership with the European Space Agency. Trepang2 (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 29Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, and PC Game Pass Become the ultimate badass in Trepang2: A gory, action-packed FPS set in the near future. Unleash all hell on your enemies, dodge bullets and leave a trail of destruction in this hardcore and frenetic shooter. Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era (Game Preview) (PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is the official prequel hailing back to the origins of the genre-defining, critically acclaimed series of turn-based strategy games. Raise grand armies and wield devastating spells to overcome your foes in both solo and multiplayer. Sledding Game (Game Preview) (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Sledding Game is a multiplayer snow-sports hangout with proximity voice chat. Race downhill, pull off ridiculous tricks, build ramps, or just relax with friends. Customize your animal avatar, jump into public or private lobbies, and enjoy chaotic ragdoll crashes — just watch out for the yeti. TerraTech Legion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Build the ultimate battle vehicle and rage against the machines in a chaotic Bullet Heaven Builder. Master a deep, block-based vehicle-building system to survive an army of robotic foes driven by a crazed AI, ramming them at speed and shooting infinite projectiles. Final Fantasy V (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – May 5Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, PC Game Pass When the crystals that balance the world are threatened, the king hastens to the rescue…only to go missing. A young man and his Chocobo find themselves drawn toward friends that will change their destiny in this classic RPG. The following games are leaving the Game Pass library soon. Jump back in to tie up any loose ends or save up to 20% off your purchase to keep the fun going! Go pick out what game you’re going to play next, and we’ll meet you back here to tell you what else is “Coming Soon”! Cheers! Note: Games with a ‘Handheld’ designation represent those that are optimized for handheld play.

title: The V: Session ends as key bills fail and primaries remain unsettled - Alabama Political Reporter
content: Filings link McKee PAC to out-of-state network, tied to donors facing charges Jones rebuts Hunt attack on immigration enforcement record Moore outraised, outspent Marshall in the first quarter of 2026 Lumpkin secures New South Alliance backing in HD 60 race Rep. Lomax contrasts tax cuts with Mo Brooks’ legislative record Gov. Ivey signs tax relief bill, state grocery tax eliminated for two months Gov. Ivey showcases healthcare legislation signed into law Governor signs act ensuring athletic eligibility for CHOOSE Act students Consumer Reports urges Ivey to veto Alabama Personal Data Protection Act Gov. Ivey signs tax relief bill, state grocery tax eliminated for two months Opinion | Investing in Alabama’s coal communities is investing in our future Alabama Cannabis Coalition reflects on mixed results from legislative session ACLU of Alabama applauds parole reforms, but warns Alabama’s prison crisis remains unaddressed Innovate Alabama launches $15M fund to retain, scale early-stage companies Small business optimism falls as oil prices fuel uncertainty Alabama Senate sends bill to limit data center tax breaks to governor Senate passes industry-to-classroom workforce tax credit bill Opelika hemp store seeks city approval as funds run out Moore outraised, outspent Marshall in the first quarter of 2026 RFK Jr., Terri Sewell clash over Black youth remarks Sen. Britt’s Stop the Scroll Act passes through Senate committee Rep. Aderholt announces winners of 2026 art competition Gov. Ivey announces record CHOOSE Act applications for 2026–27 school year Auburn student chapter wins award at national convention U.S. Navy invests $30M in Alabama maritime workforce training ASU partners with United Methodist Church, Wesley Foundation to support student development Mark Merrick named Dean of UAB School of Health Professions Gov. Ivey showcases healthcare legislation signed into law UAB Medicine expands care with new clinics in first quarter of 2026 House passes bill to prohibit vaping in public spaces, rename Alabama Clean Indoor Air Act The Shoulder launches Operation VETS to aid Alabama veterans struggling with substance abuse Cancer researchers highlight findings, push for funding at Alabama capitol Opinion | The Halifax Resolves: the first step toward independence Opinion | The ignorance is killing people in Montgomery Legislative failures, rising political tensions and unsettled primaries reveal shifting power dynamics and uncertainty shaping Alabama’s political landscape ahead. This week on “The Voice of Alabama Politics,” the Legislature closes its 2026 session, but the real story is what didn’t pass. Several high-profile bills collapsed in the final hours, including a push to close primaries, a restructuring of the Archives Board and expanded local cooperation with ICE. Together, they reveal a pattern: momentum without resolution, and proposals that faltered when final decisions had to be made. Bill Britt, Susan Britt and Josh Moon break down the biggest developments: legislative failures, a growing clash between political and religious authority on the national stage, and Republican primary races that remain wide open with little time left. Have a question or a scoop? Send us a tip. We'll track it down. The Alabama Political Reporter is a daily political news site devoted to Alabama politics. We provide accurate, reliable coverage of policy, elections and government. Federal filings show McKee-supporting PAC funded by interconnected out-of-state committees, including one linked to a nondisclosing nonprofit organization. Sheriff Jay Jones says recent claims by Cam Hunt misrepresent office’s cooperation with federal immigration authorities and enforcement practices. content: It's time to take a look at the standout performers from Section V. Your vote will determine who will be the Faber Builders Boys Sports Athlete of the Week for April 13-19. The nominees are chosen by our sports reporters. If you would like to call attention to an athlete's performance to be considered for the nomination, please email sports@democratandchronicle.com by 7 p.m. each Sunday. All athletes who play on Section V boys teams are eligible to be nominated. The poll can be found at the bottom of the article. Now, let's meet the candidates. Spencerport junior shot the best score during a golf match against Brockport, an impressive two-under par 34 on the final nine holes at Mill Creek Golf Course. Fairport senior became the first track and field athlete in Section V this season to run a 800-meter race in less than two minutes, when he crossed the finish line at the Webster Invitational in 1 minute, 59.67 seconds. A senior at Pittsford Sutherland, who shot a score of 34, an impressive one-under par, over nine holes at Caledonia Country Club. Honeoye Falls-Lima baseball player pitched a complete game (seven innings), a shutout with 12 strikeouts and two hits and had six hits in nine at-bats during the week. Victor boys lacrosse player tallied six goals with one assist in the Blue Devils’ win against Canandaigua, in a rematch of last season’s Class B section final. Batavia-Notre Dame boys lacrosse player recorded five goals and one assist while finishing 12-for-12 at the faceoff X in his team’s win against Gananda. title: Nacelle Improvement Elevates Bell Boeing V-22 Readiness - Bell Textron, Inc.
content: Explore our range of military aircraft and missions Explore our range of commercial aircraft and missions Explore our range of military aircraft and missions Amarillo, TX (April 20, 2026) – Bell Textron Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company, announced initial results and benefits from more than 10,000 flight hours of Air Force Special Operations Command (AFSOC) CV-22 Ospreys with the Nacelle Improvement (NI) Program. The first of nine CV-22s with the 20th Special Operations Squadron at Cannon Air Force Base received the NI upgrade in 2021, and the program has produced a 75 percent reduction in maintenance hours resulting in a significant boost in operational readiness and maintainability. The V-22 Osprey nacelle houses critical power components to the V-22’s vertical take-off and landing capabilities and transition to forward flight. The NI program is a targeted upgrade designed to modernize the V-22’s nacelles. By simplifying wiring, enhancing structural components, and integrating fleet maintainer-informed feedback, NI improves the Osprey’s reliability, readiness, and sustainability for any mission for the next 30 years. "Since the initial rollout, the CV-22 Nacelle Improvement has saved AFSOC more than 24,000 maintenance hours, equating to a savings of more than 1,000 days of maintainer time that can be used toward other high-priority needs,” said V-22 Joint Program Office Principal Military Deputy Program Manager and CV-22 Senior Materiel Leader. “CV-22 readiness saw more than a 10 percent increase; meaning more mission capable CV-22s on the flightline, which allows for further training and improved safety.” Bell completes the NI modification at its Amarillo Assembly Center (AAC), which actively produces V-22s for the U.S. Department of War. The AAC assembles all variants of the Bell Boeing V-22 model – MV, CMV, and CV. “The Nacelle Improvement Program enhances the V-22s reliability, flexibility, and global reach for combat and humanitarian missions alike,” said Kurt Fuller, senior vice president, Military Fielded Programs, Bell. “We are pleased to see these remarkable results from the NI program and look forward to continued collaboration to enhance focus on V-22 safety, sustainability, and readiness.” More info: Bell Boeing V-22 Nacelle Improvement Program Thinking above and beyond is what we do. For more than 90 years, we’ve been reimagining the experience of flight – and where it can take us. We're an aerospace and defense company that engineers and manufactures aircraft for critical solutions in extreme scenarios. We're breaking barriers in lifting people to safety across transportation, medical, rescue and military services, and leading the industry in future solutions that are fast, reliable and efficient. Headquartered in Fort Worth, Texas – as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Textron Inc., – we have strategic locations around the globe. And with nearly one quarter of our workforce having served, helping our military achieve their missions is a passion of ours. Above all, our breakthrough innovations deliver exceptional experiences to our customers. Efficiently. Reliably. And always, with safety at the forefront. Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services. Textron is known around the world for its powerful brands such as Bell, Cessna, Beechcraft, Hawker, Jacobsen, Kautex, Lycoming, E-Z-GO, Arctic Cat, Textron Systems, and TRU Simulation + Training. For more information, visit: www.textron.com. title: Man City - mancity.com
content: Thanks as always for your company on a huge afternoon in the Premier League. Wherever you've been following from, I hope you've enjoyed the performance and the three points. No rest for the wicked though and we go to Burnley in three days. Five games left in the Premier League with the title to play for. It's always exciting to be a Blue! Until next time... "They don't allow you to go through the process, they're so aggressive. They are good in the duels and one of the most competitive teams I ever faced in my career in terms of duels and one against one, long balls with Havertz and Rice, second balls - fantastic. "We had momentum, they had momentum, we had chances, they had chances. At the end in these type of games, it is little margins. "Both teams are good - really top managers! And really top players for both sides. "I've been so demanding but the reality is, it's just one game. They are top of the league with one goal difference [better than City]. "We have to enjoy it, celebrate it, take the good things but don't lose the focus. The focus is in three days, we go to Burnley." Very upset with the result obviously. We came here to win the game. We prepared and believed we could win it. We started a goal down but we proved that we are there. In the boxes was the difference, that’s what decided the game. The difference was there. It’s a new league now. We have three points advantage but they have a game in hand. We’re not going to stop and go again. Few sights better than Rodri in full flow. Elegance and composure on the ball, a warrior off it. Head to your Cityzens feed to enter the competition for a signed shirt. Very big. If we win the game in hand we’re on the same points. We’re happy that we can be on the same points. Erling was fantastic today fighting for every ball. Today he fought like an animal. I thought we were very good until we made the mistake and we changed the mood a bit after the 30th minute. Second half was tough, it looks like we were in control but they are always dangerous, they has two big chances. The small margins make the difference. Overall we played a good game. We were there, our fans were there, I’m proud. It’s a long season. A lot of small details didn’t go our way this season. Unlucky plays that we have had, we missed a lot of chances and today things went our way. I was quite happy I have to be honest. It was a great moment for me. A great win. I think it’s always like this, lots of fighting. What can i say, it’s always nice. I think I remember that cross when he headed it out. I just told him he was like Cannavaro today. Bernie, you were really good.

title: BTS Star V Reveals He “Tried Very Hard to Forget" About the Group to Focus On Himself - Resonate Voices
content: Kim Taehyung, known more widely as V, has spent much of his career as one of the most recognisable faces in global pop. But during BTS’ temporary pause in 2022, the singer found himself confronting a different question: who is he without it? As the group stepped away for military service, Taehyung—known to fans as V—describes a period defined less by absence, and more by intention. For the first time in years, he wasn’t performing, promoting, or moving at the relentless pace that had come to define BTS’ rise. Instead, he made a conscious decision to step back from the identity he had built. “Well, while we’re in the military, we can’t perform even if we want to,” he recalled in a recent interview. “So I did really yearn to perform. I wanted to release an album, I wanted to sing and dance. I definitely felt that … desire? Let’s call it desire. But since we were needed by our country, we had to do the best with our situation at the time. So I tried very hard to forget about V of BTS for a while, so that I could concentrate on living my life as Kim Taehyung.” Read more: BTS Star V Returns to Acting in ‘That Night, Our Decaf’, Collaborating With Compose Coffee That separation became a defining thread of his time away. Rather than hold onto the momentum of his idol persona, Taehyung leaned into something quieter—rebuilding from the inside out. “That idea was so important to me. I knew that my time in the military was going to be quite long, so I thought very hard about how to spend it well and not waste it away. First off, I considered my values and my health. I tried to organize and give a lot of thought to my inner world, looking back on myself and contemplating the kind of person I ultimately want to become.” For Taehyung, that meant stripping things back to basics. Over the course of his enlistment, he focused on routine and reflection—working out, reading, and immersing himself in music without the pressure of output. “So for that year and a half, I worked out a whole lot. Read a lot, listened to a lot of music. And I think that gave me the opportunity to rebuild my body and mind.” Books, in particular, offered a form of escape—though not always clarity. “I asked my friend: ‘Hey, I want to read! Do you have any books to recommend?’ And they got so excited and recommended Han Kang’s and Keigo Higashino’s books. So that’s literally all I read. There was also a book called Eleven Steps. It was kind of a philosophy book. But with any of the different books, I found myself putting myself into the text. Like imagining myself as a character in a novel, for example. I was so deep in my imagination at the time. Was it helpful? I’m not sure.” Still, the effects of that period are visible—not just internally, but outwardly too. “It’s just that … Actually, I found that after working out, I looked and felt different from when I didn’t work out, both on the outside and inside. So I guess I have a more confident figure.” That renewed sense of self carries into his solo work. His debut EP Layover reflects a version of Taehyung that feels deliberately unhurried, rooted in the genres he grew up with—jazz, classical, and alternative influences shaped by his childhood listening habits. Read more: BTS Album Arirang Surpasses One Billion Streams on Spotify “Layover came to me at a time when I felt the need to look back on my journey so far as V of BTS. The word ‘layover’ can mean a stop between destinations, but to me, it’s expressing that my life is not a straight path. I want to say: I can make this kind of music, but I also like many other styles.” “I love jazz, classical music, and even more alternative music, so I wanted to share that with ARMY and our listeners. I genuinely love music with all my heart, and Layover is that representation of me.” Even as BTS moves into a new chapter together, Taehyung’s perspective has shifted. The time apart didn’t weaken the group’s dynamic—it sharpened it. “Right. And since all seven of us have had a solo career, everyone’s individual ego is stronger now. I thought that might change things when we got together again, but we actually amplified each other and ended up making a better album. So I get the sense that our work is more polished than before.” For Taehyung, stepping away from “V” wasn’t about disconnecting from BTS—it was about understanding what he brings back to it. And now, with that clarity, he’s not choosing between the two. ‘Resonate’ actively encourages positive representation in the media by making your voice heard by delivering interesting articles from your own perspectives to actively engage wider audiences.

title: how table rituals by V-ZUG redefines homes at milan design week 2026 - Designboom
content: What if the kitchen of the future was defined not by its machinery, but by the poetry of human movement? At Milan Design Week 2026, Swiss household appliance manufacturer V-ZUG explores this possibility through Table Rituals, an immersive installation that transforms the domestic environment into a site of artistic performance. By blending invisible technology with sculptural architecture, the project suggests a future where our living spaces are designed to nurture presence and mindfulness, allowing the brand’s precision engineering to dissolve into the background of daily life. V-ZUG Table Rituals installation by Elisa Ossino at Milan Design Week 2026 V-ZUG’s Table Rituals installation, conceived by architect and designer Elisa Ossino, treats daily life as a primary design medium, proposing a world where human presence defines the experience. Within the serene confines of V-ZUG Studio Milano, the project prioritizes the quiet over the loud, creating a space where technology operates with silent precision in parallel to a user’s interaction. This vision of the future home focuses on the beauty of the ‘unfolding’ gesture, inviting visitors to reconsider how the frantic pace of modern living might be exchanged for a rhythmic, shared beauty found in the most basic of rituals. a moment from the Mise en Geste performance by Teatro delle Moire for V-ZUG The heart of this vision is a sculptural table that reimagines the kitchen as a stage for communal exchange and work. This continuous mineral surface serves as the global preview for Integra, V-ZUG’s invisible induction system developed with Inalco. By embedding technology directly into the material, the design achieves a calm, visually reduced architectural expression that removes the barrier between appliance and furniture. This experiment in materiality shifts the focus back to the act of making, using modern pantry objects and tableware to ground the high-tech soul of the system in a tactile, human reality. performers with textile sculptures by Matteo Idini animate V-ZUG’s installation To translate this design into a felt experience, the installation hosts Mise en Geste, a choreographed performance by Teatro delle Moire. Three performers, draped in textile sculptures by Matteo Idini, transform routine kitchen actions into a rhythmic interplay of cooking, movement, and sound. This performative sequence suggests that the evolution of design lies in opening space for true presence, where innovation and precision no longer compete for attention but instead harmonize with the movements of the human body to create a more imaginative and hopeful domestic future. the sculptural table landscape showcasing V-ZUG’s invisible technology V-ZUG Studio Milano transformed into an immersive landscape of domestic rituals a close-up of Integra, V-ZUG’s invisible induction system, at Milan Design Week 2026 V-ZUG’s appliances merge seamlessly with mineral surfaces for a calm kitchen architecture date: April 20 – 25, 2026 location: Piazza San Marco 4 artists: Elisa Ossino (Concept and Creative Direction), Teatro delle Moire (Performance), Matteo Idini (Costumes)

title: James V. Petrella Jr. 1957-2026 - vindy.com
content: GIRARD — James V. Petrella Jr., 68, passed away peacefully in the comfort of his home Sunday, April 12, 2026. James was born Nov. 15, 1957, in Youngstown, a son to the late James V. Sr. and Angeline “Jeanne” Saborse Petrella. He was a graduate of Cardinal Mooney High School and graduated from Youngstown State University. James had worked for Trumbull County and the YMCA in Youngstown. In his spare time, James enjoyed working in the yard and meeting his friends at Dunkin’. He was an avid Cleveland sports fan, but above all else, he loved his family and spending time with them. James leaves to cherish his memory, his wife of 41 years, Debra Miranda Petrella, whom he married Sept. 2, 1983; his daughter, Jenna (Michael) Costantini; brothers, Phillip (Karen) Petrella and Vince Petrella; and grandchildren, Olivia, Aiden, Arianna, Adriano and Aviana; along with many nieces and nephews. He was preceded in death by his son, James “Jimmy” Petrella, and sister, Linda Petrella. A memorial Mass will be 11 a.m. Thursday, April 23, at St. Rose Church in Girard. James will be laid to rest in Green Haven Memorial Gardens. The family would like to thank Dr. Buccino, Dr. Marina, Dr. Hoffman and the Wound Care Center in Youngstown for the care and compassion that was given to James. To view this obituary and send expressions of sympathy to the family or to share a fond memory you have of Mr. Petrella, please visit www.blackstonefuneralhome.com. title: V: ‘I Gave My All to Create This Album’ - Rolling Stone
content:
V
 spent much of his childhood with his grandmother in Geochang, a small town in southern South Korea. He came away with a taste for older music, from Chet Baker to Frank Sinatra to Elvis Presley, and a broody, smoldering vibe that stands out among his bandmates. He has a voice to match, a smoky baritone that he lets loose on his vibe-y, bedroom-eyed debut EP, Layover. He’s always been an essential element in BTS’ sound, and on Arirang, his songwriting contributions included essential tracks like “Into the Sun.” He seems quietly confident about it all, with the exception of his inability to hit the kind of high notes that come so naturally to the likes of Jimin. “I am on the lower end,” he says, in a February interview conducted in Hybe’s Seoul, South Korea headquarters. “But I’ve learned a lot. I’ve gotten better since learning from our past albums, but it will always be a challenge for me.” How did you feel in 2022, when it became clear a break was coming? This is all you’ve known for so long.Well, while we’re in the military, we can’t perform even if we want to. So I did really yearn to perform. I wanted to release an album, I wanted to sing and dance. I definitely felt that … desire? Let’s call it desire. But since we were needed by our country, we had to do the best with our situation at the time. So I tried very hard to forget about V of BTS for a while, so that I could concentrate on living my life as Kim Taehyung. You said that after discharge, you rebuilt and reset your mind and body. What does that mean to you?That idea was so important to me. I knew that my time in the military was going to be quite long, so I thought very hard about how to spend it well and not waste it away. First off, I considered my values and my health. I tried to organize and give a lot of thought to my inner world, looking back on myself and contemplating the kind of person I ultimately want to become. So for that year and a half, I worked out a whole lot. Read a lot, listened to a lot of music. And I think that gave me the opportunity to rebuild my body and mind. What’s the most important thing you read?I asked my friend: “Hey, I want to read! Do you have any books to recommend?” And they got so excited and recommended Han Kang’s and Keigo Higashino’s books. So that’s literally all I read. There was also a book called Eleven Steps. It was kind of a philosophy book. But with any of the different books, I found myself putting myself into the text. Like imagining myself as a character in a novel, for example. I was so deep in my imagination at the time. Was it helpful? I’m not sure. ARMY says you seem more confident. Do you feel that way?It’s just that … Actually, I found that after working out, I looked and felt different from when I didn’t work out, both on the outside and inside. So I guess I have a more confident figure. You are so careful with your solo music. You have a lot of songs that you make, and then you don’t put out or you delete. What did it mean to get Layover finished and out into the world?Layover came to me at a time when I felt the need to look back on my journey so far as V of BTS. The word “layover” can mean a stop between destinations, but to me, it’s expressing that my life is not a straight path. I want to say: I can make this kind of music, but I also like many other styles. I love jazz, classical music, and even more alternative music, so I wanted to share that with ARMY and our listeners. I genuinely love music with all my heart, and Layover is that representation of me. The new BTS album is very different from your solo work. Going into it, what were your personal goals for the album?First and most importantly, there’s not a single song on this album that is not my style. Every single one is my style and the kind of music I’ve wanted to make. I think this was another color I wanted to pursue, in terms of genre. So I gave it my all to create this album, and it has a lot of love in it. This Arirang album, I mean. Do you have a favorite moment, whether your own or from another member, on this album?Since we worked on so many songs, there were plenty of songs I loved that didn’t get picked for the album. And of course I have my favorites among the songs that got included. But more than anything … I can’t wait to be onstage again. Because my favorite songs always change when we get to perform. In a group of seven members, there’s only so much sonic real estate. Does ego ever get involved — “I would like to be more on this song,” or “This song where I’m featured more didn’t make it”?Well, I think I was more worried about being able to do a good job. Because no matter how much I listen to what I like, it’s a totally different game to perform it myself. So even if I love something, I have to try very, very hard to interpret and reimagine it in a way that makes sense for me to do. But if I ever have fewer parts or I’m taking a less central part in a song, I don’t feel bad about that. Because another member will take that place. I just feel … relieved, in a way. The only thing is, if the seven of us don’t mesh, we just pass on that song. I think the group is good at making everyone feel pretty much equal. Right. And since all seven of us have had a solo career, everyone’s individual ego is stronger now. I thought that might change things when we got together again, but we actually amplified each other and ended up making a better album. So I get the sense that our work is more polished than before. Would it be accurate to say you have zero desire to be a solo pop star?If I have zero desire, shouldn’t I stop being an artist? I learned so much from [the other members] while working on this album and got a lot out of observing their paths. I really learned a lot. So when the time comes to make my next solo album, I plan to absorb every member’s biggest strengths. I love the jazzier sound on something like the solo track “Winter Ahead,” which is a really beautiful song. But it sounds like you may be open to making a pop album.Yeah. Even that is a style of music I love — one that I’ve worked to pursue. So, of course. I don’t know when that would be, but someday, it’s a genre that I’d have to and would love to try. That’s what I think. ARMY loves your acting, but they would like you to play a part where the character doesn’t die. Will you do more of it, and will you seek out a part where the character gets to live?I think my mindset is the same in my acting as in my music. When I’m acting, part of the point is to try a bunch of different parts that I can’t experience in reality, right? So in the same way, I don’t want to limit myself to one genre of music. I want to experience every single genre I really love. And someday, if I get the chance, I hope I can act in a lot of different parts that I’ve wanted to try out. If there’s one jazz artist you want ARMY to get into, who would it be?Oh, there’s too many! Etta James! Styling by YEJIN KIM. Hair by HANSOM, HWAYEON, and HYUNWOO LEE. Makeup by DAREUM KIM and SHINAE.Set design by YEABYUL JEON. Produced by NUHANA. Executive Producer SOOH HWANG. Producers SEBIN PARK and KALY NGO. Line producer CHERRY LEE. Digital Technician HUIJIN KIM. Photographic Assistance: SOOJUNG OH, MINHYUK LEE, MINJUN KIM, JIHYUN OH, JUWAN KANG and JUNHYUNG YANG. Set Design Team: SOHYUN WON, YUNSEON CHOI, JUNHYUK SIM. RS Video DoP MIKE BEECH. Camera Operators: BYEONG HWI MIN, CHURL GWON, HYUNSUH PAIK. DIT JIWOON LEE. Sound operator MIN JAE LEE. Production assistant SEOHYUN YOONClothing CreditsV: Jacket by BONBOM. Sweater by SSSTEIN. Pants by MAISON MARGIELA. We want to hear it. Send us a tip using our anonymous form. Rolling Stone is a part of Penske Media Corporation. © 2026 Rolling Stone, LLC. title: Apollo v Artemis: How the Earth changed in 58 years - BBC
content: After the Apollo 8 crew captured the iconic Earthrise photo in 1968, Artemis astronauts have recreated the image, revealing changes to our fragile blue planet. When the commander of Apollo 8, Frank Borman, first saw the far side of the Moon from his spacecraft window in 1968 he was struck by its desolate appearance. "The lunar surface was terribly distressed with meteorite craters and volcanic residue," he told me during a BBC interview in 2018. "It was either grey or black or white – there was absolutely no colour on the lunar surface, and it was messed up beyond belief." But as the spacecraft completed its fourth lunar orbit, suddenly, a very different view appeared. "We looked up and there was the Earth in the background coming over the lunar surface, and Bill Anders took the picture that became probably one of the more significant pictures that humans have ever taken," Borman said. "The Earth was the only thing in the entire Universe that had any colour, it was a remarkable sight – we're very, very fortunate to live on this planet." This Earthrise picture, as it was soon named, became one of the most reproduced images of all time. Showing our planet in the context of the lunar wasteland and vast oblivion of space, it galvanised the environmental movement, leading to the creation of Earth Day in 1970. (Read more about the 1968 photo that changed the world). Fifty-eight years later, Nasa astronauts have taken another striking photo of the Earth dipping below a barren lunar landscape: "Earthset". During their fly-by of the Moon earlier this month, the Artemis II crew captured the new image of our fragile blue planet in the vast expanse of space. (We don't know who took the picture this time because the four astronauts have chosen not to credit photos to individuals but to the whole crew). In geological terms, just over half a century is barely the shadow of a blink of an eye. But climate change has altered the Earth's surface significantly over the past six decades. Experts tell the BBC about the visible differences between the Earthrise and Earthset photos and what these tell us about our planet then and now. Despite its impact and legacy, what's so surprising about the Earthrise picture is that no one at Nasa had seen it coming. "They caught it by accident, right?" says US astronaut Sian Proctor, the pilot for the first wholly "civilian" mission to space (named Inspiration). "Apollo 8 changed the way we saw our planet and that's the kind of thing I feel like we need right now, more inspiration." When I asked a question at the press conference following the Artemis launch about plans for a new Earthrise picture, it was clear that, this time, Nasa was not going to be caught out. "We're going to do everything we can to try and make that happen," said Lori Glaze, who leads the agency's Exploration Systems Development Mission Directorate. The Earthset photo was captured through the Orion spacecraft window 18:41 Eastern Daylight Time (23:41BST) on 6 April, during a seven-hour flyby of the Moon. "The Earth's sunlit side shows white clouds and blue water over the Oceania region, while the dark areas are experiencing nighttime. The image also shows incredible detail of the Moon's surface and its overlapping craters and basins," Nasa notes in its description of the image. Unlike in 1968, numerous satellites take thousands of images of our planet each day in 2026. They measure and monitor our oceans, land and ice in all shades of the electromagnetic spectrum – from microwave to ultraviolet – not just what we can see with our eyes. There is a continuous video feed from the International Space Station and robotic spacecraft have even captured the Earth from the vantage point of the Moon and beyond, but the fact that Earthset was taken by humans sets it apart. Craig Donlon, who oversees the plans for the next generation of satellites at the European Space Agency (Esa), says humans give us a different perspective. "The images that you get from a human are framed, focused, and there are choices that are made subconsciously and consciously by the astronaut when they press the shutter, they've got something in the back of their mind," Donlon says. "That forces some kind of emotion, which says, 'Well, wow, okay, little old Earth there, but that's where we live, that's it, that's everything.'" But it's not just the human connection that makes the Earthrise and Earthset images significant. Despite being snapshots taken some 58 years apart, they can also help reveal how the Earth has changed in that time. "Since Earthrise, atmospheric carbon dioxide levels have risen by about a third and global temperatures have warmed rapidly, by at least 1C," says Richard Allan, a professor of climate science at the National Centre for Earth Observation at the University of Reading in the UK. "The planet has transformed as human activities alter the texture of our land as seen from space, expanding cities, clearing dark forests for replacement by bright agricultural lands and contributing to the desiccation of the Aral Sea which has shrunk to less than 10% of its 1960s size." Some of that change can even be seen in the images themselves, even though the Earth is shrouded in cloud. "Although they're of different parts of the Earth, the one thing that does show up in both images, although you're looking at different parts of it, is Antarctica and the Southern Ocean," says Benjamin Wallis, a glaciologist at the University of Leeds in the UK. "The Antarctic Peninsula is one of the most rapidly warming parts of the Earth and 28,000km of ice shelf have collapsed in between the original image and the latest image." Studies suggest these changes in the ice around Antarctica are unprecedented in the last 10,000 years. Other areas of the Earth where water exists in a solid form – known as the cryosphere – have been similarly affected. "We really have seen some dramatic changes," says Petra Heil, director of science at the British Antarctic Survey. "We have now seen in both hemispheres a dramatic decrease in the seasonal sea ice cover, and in North America, Eurasia and Asia, we have seen much later seasonal snow cover, and we also see it melting earlier." "I think we are pretty confident based on the observations but also the numerical models to assign probably 90-95% of the change to human activities," Heil says. But while all this might make for grim reading, it's worth remembering that in 1968 – despite appearances from deep space – we had already caused damage to the planet. • Mercury to Artemis: The evolution of mission control "[Earthrise] left many people enthralled with the idea of how beautiful the Earth was and how much damage was being done," says Kathleen Rogers, president of the Earth Day network. "I remember at the time you couldn't see across the street in LA at rush hour because of smog and our rivers were on fire." "From so far away, the Earth looks so perfect and so beautiful and then as you drew closer, you could see the ravages of 150 years of, let's call it, progress," says Rogers. "But it did inspire a generation of people to really step it up and become part of a movement." Borman died in 2023, but the legacy of his Apollo 8 mission still endures and his words are just as relevant today for a new generation of lunar astronauts: "I don't think any of us have paid any attention to the fact that we would be going all the way to the Moon and be more interested in looking at the Earth". For essential climate news and hopeful developments to your inbox, sign up to the Future Earth newsletter, while The Essential List delivers a handpicked selection of features and insights twice a week. For more science, technology, environment and health stories from the BBC, follow us on Facebook and Instagram. With storms occurring between 140 to 160 nights a year, it's no wonder the area is a world record holder. title: V.com weekend vote: At what age did you start playing the violin? - Violinist.com
content: We have thousands of human-written stories, discussions, interviews and reviews from today through the past 20+ years. Find them here: April 18, 2026, 7:52 PM · Last week an article about starting the violin as a senior citizen inspired a robust conversation about people's experiences, starting the violin at an advanced age. It would seem that quite a lot of people have started playing their instrument late in life, so I wanted to re-do a vote we've done before: at what age did you start playing the violin? I have added a "senior citizen" category: people who started over the age of 65. It's a great chance to talk about when all of us started, and I predict that the answers will be all over the map! Since the advent of the Suzuki method, it's not unusual to start at a very young age, even two or three. But you can enjoy this instrument starting at any age, and with the key ingredients of motivation and consistent practice, you can get quite good at it. Personally, I started violin a few days shy of my ninth birthday, in a public school program at Eastridge Elementary School in Aurora, Colorado. Thank you Mrs. Linda Walker, for inspiring young me to beg my parents to join your program! How old were you when you started playing the violin (or viola), and what was that initial spark that got you going? Please participate in the vote, and then tell us all about it in the comments! April 19, 2026 at 12:10 AM · I started violin when I was 7, but I honestly don't remember why. I think someone suggested it, but I honestly don't recall. I had done piano for a few years by that point. I ended up loving both piano and violin and took lessons on both throughout grade school. I also added viola when I was 12. April 19, 2026 at 01:30 AM · I was 7yrs old when I started. My parents took me to a local concert. I remember being mesmerized by the sound of the violin, and how much I enjoyed watching the emotions displayed by the violinists while playing. I also remember enjoying watching the conductor conduct.. lol. After the concert, I asked my parents if I can learn to play the violin, a few weeks later, my Mom introduced me to my first Suzuki violin teacher, and the rest is history. Sometimes I wonder if that was the intent of my parents all along.. lol. Sidenote: My kids started to learn the violin when they turned 3 1/2 yrs old with a Suzuki trained teacher. They wanted to learn because they hear me play all their lives, and was attracted to the sound of the instrument and music in general. April 19, 2026 at 01:35 AM · I started at 5, stopped after high school and started picking it up about a  years and half ago. It's been very slow picking it back up with no time to practice but I hope to practice more and with a purpose now that I have my dream violin and a pretty good bow. April 19, 2026 at 07:01 AM · Aged 11 I started at a boarding school and was given a shotgun choice between the violin and the recorder (which of course is a girl's instrument so no choice really). At a day school (or at a later age) I wouldn't have had that compulsion and today I'd be a completely different person. April 19, 2026 at 10:36 AM · I started playing the fiddle at 60... but moved back to playing classical music at about aged 64.It was a much bigger challenged than I expected but 8 years later I am really enjoying playing second violin in a local orchestra, and in a small strings chamber group. I had played the flute from aged 9, then the oboe, piano, wooden traditional flute and concertina, all instruments with reasonable set or fixed intonation. Nothing prepared me for the difficulty of learning to play the violin in tune, but it has been one of the most rewarding experiences of my life. April 19, 2026 at 12:17 PM · I requested during the school year of my 9th birthday, started lessons on my 10th. Most other people in my class wanted to play the guitar, so me wanting to be different, chose the violin. At the time, I didn't realise how undifferent it was lmao. April 19, 2026 at 01:19 PM · I started at 58 motivated by Western Swing, a declining uke group, Prok 2, Bartok's string quartets (Guarneri quartet), an interest in classical music since I was 5 and the realisation that violin sections are the biggest orchestral sections, increasing my chances of finding a place. April 19, 2026 at 01:55 PM · Both my parents were violinists, and a baby class was offered at the University of Texas, Austin. I vividly remember playing on the stage at the concert when I was 2. April 19, 2026 at 05:23 PM · Reply{#9} Elisabeth Matesky, "Apostle" of Jascha Heifetz and Nathan Milstein, Yet I began playing the Violin on my *Third Birthday with Poppa Ralph Matesky, Juilliard Top of Class Grad & honoured Violin String Teacher for an entire District of major City Schools Music Programmes & whom Laurie Niles knows via her 1st acquaintances with the Violin & I believe other String Cousins!! I was inspired by both my parents & professional musicians highly regarded throughout the major city they lived in when hearing/listening at 2 to Momma on the Piano (Arnold Schoenberg's UCLA Advanced Classes Pianist) and Poppa, Superb Violinist/Pupil of both Piastro and Ysaye 'Apostle' Eduoard Dethier at the Juilliard School of Music, with both reading through Violin & Piano Sonatas at home hearing beautiful sounds aged 2. I am told and was forever informed I loved the Piano and Violin asking for a "Biolin" (unable at aged 2 to pronounce Violin properly) and on my Third Birthday gifted a baby violin in a black case with a doted top of the violin case with a beautiful green colour inside where my new baby violin sat with a bow. I can still See the Scene in my mind & even now knowing it was so special a Birthday to me, just a child yet loving music of my parents who always read Brahms, Mozart and LvB plus other Composers unknown to me at that early time in my little life, but loving the sounds and watching beloved parents making music together and it always seemed with Love! I never recall an argument between my parents over Music: Never. Their Example brought me much Joy as I started growing up and wanting to play in the elementary school orchestra which I had to audition or "Try Out" for and then invited to become a Member of the Second Violins Last Stand and truly thrilled to be a part of an orchestra! I learnt many Violin II Orchestral Parts which was So Good for a child with Big Ears and Love for Music which I truly do believe to This Writing Is Good for All People and also now discovering by Autism Experts Health Aide for Children diagnosed with a form of severe "non verbal" unable autism who cannot communicate with words But 'if learning an Instrument Can Learn to "Feel and Love Sounds" on a musical instrument and can learn to Play a Violin if taught with great Love and Patience plus some sort of Common Sense backgrounds, Musically-Medically. Only Yesterday, I was reading an Article about this Subject and now inspired having met last month a Mom with a severely non verbal child of 9 with Autism who needs major Help to Self-Help her beloved Child and this site for Strings is a boost Today with many replying and I am sure More to come! Re: Learning to play a violin or String Cousin (Viola, Violoncello or Double Bass or Classical Guitar) over 65, I think a very healthy approach to getting older or succinctly put: "More Mature!" for those intrigued or just plain curious about How to Play the Violin or other String Cousins here mentioned!! It is Not an Activity to be Judged by any Teacher. This is a paramount Ingredient to Start Day One with and to Not try to "Teach" Every Thing in a First Day Lesson/ First Acquaintance with a person 65 or Over even 80 or 90!!!!! The Idea is to Encourage and with tons of Knowledge but Not to foist all knowledge on a beginner who has lived on Planet Earth for some time and often longer than a String Teacher interested or curious to Try to teach an older person! I do believe Music is for Every Person on Earth and our glorious Ages Old Composers for Every Generation and depending on their place of Birth re Specific Culture if not exposed to Violin Playing in the "Traditional" Way most Western Countries know it to be but for adaptations of such if from Countries with Ages Old Instruments resembling Western Violins and the playing of all Violins made in many Centuries but when starting out best to Rent a Violin even if aged 65 or Older to Not incur major Costs and unknown unless a very experienced Violin or other String Cousin Teacher is there and to Not make 'A Deal" to get a certain Percentage Back from a Said String Dealer or Shop.
